probate-attorney-downtown-houston-tx.heroIntro: Downtown Houston is the heart of Harris County — and home to the four probate courts where every probate matter for residents of Houston's central core is heard. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has filed and resolved probate cases at 201 Caroline Street for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-downtown-houston-tx.localContent:
Downtown Houston has changed dramatically over the last two decades. Where there were once mostly office towers, residential life has returned in force — Skyhouse, Market Square Tower, One Park Place, the Houston House, the Star, and the lofts in the Theater and Warehouse Districts now house thousands of residents. With that residential growth has come a recurring set of probate questions: how does a high-rise condo transfer? What about a parking deed? What if I work in oil and gas and have stock options that vested but didn't pay out? We see these issues regularly for downtown clients.

The good news for Downtown residents is that the courthouse is right there. The four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts sit at 201 Caroline Street, in the Civil Courthouse just north of Discovery Green. Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 each have their own judge and docket; cases are randomly assigned at filing. Whether you live in a Theater District loft, a Market Square high-rise, or a Midtown townhome, your case will land in one of those four courts.

If you've recently lost a family member, are serving as executor of a Downtown resident's estate, or want to put a plan in place that keeps your assets out of probate, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Downtown Houston Residents

Probate is the court-supervised process of settling a deceased person's estate. We handle the full range of Texas probate work for Downtown clients:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — for estates with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for downtown condo owners
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will, to establish legal heirs under Texas law
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Downtown Houston Residents

Downtown residents often have a particular asset profile — a condo, brokerage and retirement accounts, sometimes employer stock or RSUs, and frequently family or property in another state. A solid estate plan ties all of those together. We help Downtown clients with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — especially useful for residents who own real estate outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your condo or townhome out of probate entirely

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. If you believe a will was signed under undue influence, lack of capacity, or fraud — or if a trustee or executor is mishandling estate assets — there's a two-year statutory window to contest, and earlier action almost always strengthens the case.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims on behalf of beneficiaries — denied claims, contestability disputes, interpleader actions — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Downtown and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout Downtown Houston, Midtown, EaDo, the Museum District, and all surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Downtown Houston residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are all located at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, in the Harris County Civil Courthouse just north of Discovery Green. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing.

localFaqs.question: I live in a downtown high-rise condo. Does it have to go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on how the title is held. A condo titled solely in the deceased's name with no Transfer on Death Deed will need probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding to transfer. A condo held jointly with right of survivorship, or covered by a recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed, can transfer outside of probate. Reviewing your deed and beneficiary designations now is one of the simplest steps in estate planning.

localFaqs.question: How quickly can I get a probate matter heard in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Initial probate hearings in Harris County are typically scheduled 2 to 4 weeks after the application is filed, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. After the hearing, an uncontested independent administration generally closes in 4 to 8 months.

localFaqs.question: Are downtown condo HOAs ever a complication in probate?
localFaqs.answer: They can be. HOA fees and assessments continue to accrue during probate, and most condo associations require notice of the owner's death and proof of executor authority before they'll communicate with the family. We help executors navigate that notice process and protect against late fees or liens during administration.

probate-attorney-midtown-houston-tx.heroIntro: Midtown Houston is a high-rise neighborhood of young professionals, condo towers, and townhomes packed between Downtown and the Museum District. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has helped Midtown residents navigate Harris County probate and estate planning for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-midtown-houston-tx.localContent:
Midtown sits just south of Downtown along Main Street, bracketed by Highway 59 and Spur 527, and over the last twenty years it has become one of the densest residential pockets in Houston. The neighborhood is built around mid-rise and high-rise condos — Camden, 2016 Main, the Calais, Mosaic, and dozens more — alongside the townhome rows that fill in the streets between Bagby and Almeda. Most of our Midtown clients are working professionals in their 30s and 40s, many without children, and a sizable share have moved here from out of state for energy, medical, or finance careers. That demographic mix shapes the probate and estate planning issues we see: condos titled in a single name, retirement and brokerage accounts that dwarf the home equity, and family ties anchored in another state where the rest of the estate may sit.

Because Midtown lies inside the City of Houston and Harris County, probate matters for residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown, a five-minute drive up Main from most Midtown addresses. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing, and each judge runs a slightly different docket. Knowing how each court handles its calendar is part of how we keep uncontested probate moving for our Midtown clients.

Whether you've recently lost a parent or partner, are serving as executor on an estate that crosses state lines, or want to put a Texas Transfer on Death Deed in place to keep your condo out of probate, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Midtown Residents

Probate is the court-supervised process of settling a deceased person's estate. We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for Midtown families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — a faster, lower-cost option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for Midtown condo owners
  • Determination of Heirship — used when there is no valid will, to establish heirs under Texas intestate succession
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors and administrators on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Midtown Professionals

The classic Midtown asset profile — a condo, a 401(k), a brokerage account, and family in another state — is one of the easiest to plan well, and one of the easiest to leave a mess. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful when you own real estate outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Midtown condo or townhome out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for retirement and brokerage accounts

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

If you believe a will was signed under undue influence, lack of capacity, or fraud, we represent contestants and proponents in the Harris County Probate Courts. We also represent beneficiaries who suspect a trustee or executor has breached fiduciary duty. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate, and earlier action almost always strengthens the case.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Midtown beneficiaries — denied claims, contestability disputes, and interpleader actions — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Midtown and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout Midtown, Downtown, EaDo, the Museum District, Montrose, Upper Kirby, and surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. When you work with us, you work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Which Harris County Probate Court will hear my case if I live in Midtown?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County has four statutory probate courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown, less than two miles from most Midtown addresses. Your case is randomly assigned to one of the four courts at the time of filing; you don't choose. We file regularly in all four and know how each court runs its docket.

localFaqs.question: I own a Midtown condo titled solely in my name — what happens to it when I die?
localFaqs.answer: A condo held solely in your name with no Transfer on Death Deed and no living trust will need to pass through probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding before the deed can be transferred. If you record a Texas Transfer on Death Deed naming a beneficiary, or hold the condo jointly with right of survivorship, title can pass outside probate. For Midtown owners with no spouse or children locally, the TOD deed is one of the simplest tools to keep the unit out of court.

localFaqs.question: I'm 35 and single with no kids — do I really need an estate plan?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, and arguably more than someone with a spouse and children. Without a will, Texas intestate succession sends your assets to your parents or siblings in shares set by statute — which is rarely what young professionals actually want. A simple will, a medical power of attorney, and a durable power of attorney usually cover the gaps. We do a lot of these for Midtown clients and they don't take long to put in place.

localFaqs.question: My parent died in another state but had a brokerage account at a Houston firm — does that need Texas probate?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no. The primary probate happens where the decedent lived, and most brokerage firms will release the account to a duly-appointed out-of-state executor with letters testamentary from the home-state court. Texas ancillary probate is usually only triggered by Texas real property held in the decedent's individual name. We can review the asset list and tell you whether ancillary probate is needed at all.

probate-attorney-eado-houston-tx.heroIntro: EaDo — East Downtown — has gone from warehouse district to one of Houston's fastest-changing neighborhoods in under a decade. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC works with longtime EaDo families and new loft owners alike on Harris County probate and estate planning, with over 25 years of experience.
probate-attorney-eado-houston-tx.localContent:
EaDo sits east of Downtown across U.S. 59, anchored by Minute Maid Park, BBVA Stadium, and a corridor of converted warehouses, breweries, and new-construction townhomes that have replaced much of the old industrial fabric. The neighborhood's character is genuinely mixed: longtime working-class families who have owned homes here for two or three generations live alongside new arrivals in $600,000 townhomes and warehouse-conversion lofts. That split shapes our probate practice for EaDo. On one side, we see straightforward administrations of recently purchased homes with clean title. On the other, we see Determination of Heirship cases on family homes where the owner of record has been deceased for years and title was never cleared, often complicating a long-overdue sale or refinance.

EaDo lies inside Harris County, so probate matters for residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown, a short hop across U.S. 59. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing, and each judge runs a slightly different docket. We file regularly in all four.

If you're administering an estate, trying to clear title on a family-owned home that never went through probate, or putting an estate plan in place to protect what you've built, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. localFaqs.question: Where does an EaDo probate case get filed?
localFaqs.answer: EaDo is in Harris County, so probate cases are filed with the Harris County Clerk and heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. The case is randomly assigned to Probate Court No. 1, 2, 3, or 4 at filing.

localFaqs.question: Our family has owned a home in EaDo since the 1970s but Grandma's name is still on the deed — can we sell it?
localFaqs.answer: Not until title is cleared. When a homeowner dies and the deed is never updated through probate, the property remains titled in the deceased's name, and a buyer's title company won't insure the sale. The fix is usually a Determination of Heirship — a court proceeding that establishes who the legal heirs are under Texas law and clears the way to record a corrected deed. We handle these for longtime EaDo families regularly, and the work is often less expensive and less complicated than people assume.

localFaqs.question: I just bought a new-construction townhome in EaDo. Do I need a will?
localFaqs.answer: Yes — and a Transfer on Death Deed for the townhome is worth considering alongside the will. Without either, your home will need probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding before it can be transferred to whoever inherits it. A simple will plus a recorded TOD deed is one of the lowest-cost, highest-value pieces of planning we do, and it usually takes a single appointment.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: For an uncontested independent administration, expect roughly 4 to 8 months from filing to closing — depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to and the complexity of the estate. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

probate-attorney-montrose-houston-tx.heroIntro: Montrose is one of Houston's most distinctive neighborhoods — historic bungalows, art galleries, the Menil Collection, and a long-standing LGBTQ+ community whose families bring particular estate planning needs. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served Montrose residents through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-montrose-houston-tx.localContent:
Montrose stretches west from Downtown between Buffalo Bayou and Bissonnet, anchored by the Menil Collection, the Rothko Chapel, the historic bungalows along Westheimer, and the cluster of galleries and restaurants between Montrose Boulevard and Shepherd. The neighborhood has a deep cultural history as the heart of Houston's LGBTQ+ community and an art-and-design district, and that history still shapes who lives here. Many Montrose households are unmarried partners, married same-sex couples, blended families from previous marriages, and longtime owners of historic bungalows that have appreciated dramatically. Each of those situations calls for careful estate planning.

Texas recognizes same-sex marriage following Obergefell, so a married spouse — same-sex or otherwise — has the same intestate rights and the same access to community-property protections as any other surviving spouse. But unmarried partners are treated as legal strangers under Texas intestate succession, regardless of how long they have lived together or shared a home. Without a will, a Transfer on Death Deed, joint title with right of survivorship, or a beneficiary designation, an unmarried partner can find themselves locked out of an estate they helped build. Putting those documents in place is one of the most important pieces of planning we do for Montrose clients.

Probate matters for Montrose residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing. If you're administering a partner's or spouse's estate, contesting a will, or putting a plan in place that protects your partner and your home, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Montrose Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for Inner Loop families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — a faster option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for paid-down Montrose bungalows
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will, to establish heirs under Texas law
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Montrose Couples and Families

The right plan for a Montrose household depends on whether the partners are married, who is on the deed, and what each person wants for the other. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful for unmarried partners who want to provide for each other and avoid probate disputes with biological family
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives — important for partners who want each other empowered to make decisions in an emergency
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps a Montrose home out of probate and out of any family fight over the deed
  • Beneficiary designation review for retirement, life insurance, and brokerage accounts

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

We represent contestants and proponents in will contests at the Harris County Probate Courts. We also represent partners and spouses who suspect a stepchild or sibling is contesting in bad faith, or who suspect a trustee or executor has breached fiduciary duty. The two-year statutory contest window matters; earlier action strengthens the case.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Montrose beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Montrose and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout Montrose, Neartown, the Museum District, Upper Kirby, River Oaks, and surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: I'm in a long-term unmarried partnership in Montrose — what happens to our home if my partner dies without a will?
localFaqs.answer: Under Texas intestate succession, an unmarried partner inherits nothing automatically — the home and other assets pass to the deceased's parents, siblings, or children, in shares set by statute. If only one partner is on the deed, the surviving partner can be left without legal title. The fix is straightforward: a will leaving the home to the partner, or a Transfer on Death Deed, or joint title with right of survivorship, or a revocable living trust. We do these for Montrose couples regularly.

localFaqs.question: We're a married same-sex couple — does Texas treat us the same as any other married couple for probate?
localFaqs.answer: Yes. After Obergefell v. Hodges, Texas recognizes same-sex marriages on the same terms as any other marriage. A surviving spouse has the same intestate rights, the same homestead protections, the same community property treatment, and the same access to independent administration as any other surviving spouse. The probate court does not distinguish.

localFaqs.question: I own a historic Montrose bungalow that's appreciated hugely — does it have to go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Not necessarily. If you record a Texas Transfer on Death Deed naming a beneficiary, hold the home jointly with right of survivorship, or place the home in a revocable living trust, title passes outside probate. If you hold it solely in your name with none of those in place, your beneficiaries will need probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding to transfer it. Reviewing the deed is something we can do in a free consultation.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Montrose residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are all located at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a five-minute drive from most Montrose addresses. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing. We file regularly in all four.

probate-attorney-museum-district-houston.heroIntro: The Museum District wraps around Hermann Park in the heart of the Inner Loop — twenty museums, the medical center next door, and a residential mix from historic homes to newer townhomes. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served Museum District residents through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-museum-district-houston.localContent:
The Museum District is the cultural heart of Houston, anchored by the Museum of Fine Arts, the Houston Museum of Natural Science, the Holocaust Museum, the Children's Museum, and Hermann Park itself. The residential streets that thread through the district — along Caroline, Fannin, San Jacinto, and the side streets between Bissonnet and Hermann Park — hold a layered mix of housing: pre-war bungalows, mid-century cottages, mid-rise condos along Main, and high-end townhomes built in the last fifteen years. Many residents work in the adjacent Texas Medical Center or in academic and cultural institutions, and a meaningful share of our Museum District clients are physicians, professors, and longtime homeowners whose property values have appreciated several times over since they bought.

That mix produces two recurring estate planning patterns. The first is the longtime owner of a small historic home that is now worth far more than the rest of the estate combined — where careful planning around the homestead, capital gains step-up, and Transfer on Death Deeds matters. The second is the medical or academic professional with significant retirement savings, employer life insurance, and sometimes property in another state from a prior career, who needs a plan that ties everything together. We work with both regularly.

Probate matters for Museum District residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown, a short drive up Main Street from the district. Cases are randomly assigned among the four courts at filing. Whether you're administering a parent's estate, contesting a will, or putting a plan in place, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for Inner Loop families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — for estates with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for longtime Museum District homeowners
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Museum District Professionals and Homeowners

A solid plan ties together your home, retirement, and any out-of-state property. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for owners with property in multiple states or significant brokerage holdings
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your home out of probate
  • Charitable bequests, where appropriate — many Museum District residents want to plan for the institutions next door

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Museum District beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Museum District residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 sit at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a ten-minute drive from the Museum District up Main Street. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing.

localFaqs.question: We've owned our Museum District home since the 1980s and it's worth far more than anything else in our estate. How should we plan for it?
localFaqs.answer: The combination of a Texas Transfer on Death Deed, an updated will, and a current beneficiary designation review usually does the job for owners in your situation. The TOD deed transfers the home outside probate to the named beneficiary, and the heirs receive a stepped-up cost basis at your death — generally meaning little or no capital gains tax if the home is sold soon after. We can sit down and walk through the specifics for your property.

localFaqs.question: I'm a Texas Medical Center physician with retirement accounts and an employer life insurance policy. Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no. Retirement accounts and life insurance pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning they bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. Problems arise when the named beneficiary is an ex-spouse, a deceased parent, or was never updated after a major life change. Reviewing your beneficiary designations is one of the highest-value pieces of estate planning we do for medical professionals.

localFaqs.question: I want to leave a charitable bequest to a Museum District institution. How do I do that?
localFaqs.answer: The simplest tool is a charitable bequest in your will — a fixed dollar amount, a percentage of the estate, or a specific asset. For larger gifts, a charitable remainder trust or a beneficiary designation on a retirement account can be more tax-efficient. We work with clients to structure the gift the way they want it remembered.

probate-attorney-medical-center-houston-.heroIntro: The Texas Medical Center is the largest medical complex in the world — and the residents who work there bring a particular set of estate planning needs, from international ties to deferred compensation to frequent moves. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served TMC professionals through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-medical-center-houston-.localContent:
The Texas Medical Center anchors a residential community of physicians, nurses, researchers, postdocs, fellows, and administrative staff who work in MD Anderson, Houston Methodist, Memorial Hermann, Texas Children's, Baylor College of Medicine, UTHealth, and dozens of other institutions. Many TMC residents live in the apartment towers and condos along Main and Holcombe, in the Museum District next door, or in Braeswood and the West University area within easy commuting distance. The professional profile is unusual: high earners with significant retirement savings and often deferred compensation; a meaningful share of international or out-of-state employees who have arrived for fellowships or faculty appointments; and a population that moves more often than the Houston average, sometimes leaving real property behind in another state or country.

Each of those facts shapes the estate planning work we do for TMC clients. International residents may need both a Texas will and a coordinated will in their home country, and may face questions about whether U.S. estate tax applies to non-resident assets. Out-of-state employees often own a home back home — meaning that without a trust, their family will face two separate probates. And physicians with deferred comp, supplemental retirement accounts, and substantial life insurance typically benefit from a beneficiary designation review more than from anything else.

Probate matters for Medical Center residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — for estates with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Ancillary Probate — for international or out-of-state estates with Texas property
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court

Estate Planning for Medical Center Professionals

For TMC professionals, the highest-value planning is often the simplest. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament — coordinated with any will in the client's home country, where applicable
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful for clients with property in another state or country
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your TMC-area home or condo out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for retirement, deferred comp, supplemental life insurance, and HSAs

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

Group employer life insurance carriers sometimes deny or delay legitimate beneficiary claims, particularly when contestability period issues or beneficiary designation disputes arise. We pursue contested life insurance claims for TMC beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

Serving the Medical Center and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout the Texas Medical Center, the Museum District, Braeswood, West University, Bellaire, and surrounding neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

localFaqs.question: I'm a TMC physician on a fellowship from another country. Do I need a Texas will?
localFaqs.answer: If you own assets in Texas — a vehicle, a brokerage account, or a home — a Texas will is generally a good idea, even if you also have a will in your home country. The two need to be coordinated so they don't contradict each other or accidentally revoke each other. We work with international fellows and faculty regularly and can put a Texas-specific will in place that complements your existing planning.

localFaqs.question: I have an employer life insurance policy through my hospital. If I die during the contestability period, can the carrier deny the claim?
localFaqs.answer: Carriers do sometimes deny claims during the two-year contestability period, citing alleged misstatements on the application. Many of those denials are not legally defensible. We represent beneficiaries on contested life insurance claims — often on a contingency basis — and force a thorough review of the underwriting file. If the application was accurate, the claim usually pays.

localFaqs.question: I own a home in another state from before I moved to Houston. Do I need a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes. Real property in another state, titled in your individual name, typically triggers a separate "ancillary probate" in that state when you die — meaning your family runs two probates, one in Texas and one back home. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that. We can review your situation and tell you whether a trust is the right move.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for residents in TMC apartment buildings?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Your case is randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing. The drive from the TMC area is about ten to fifteen minutes depending on traffic.

probate-attorney-rice-village-houston-tx.heroIntro: Rice Village sits next to Rice University and West University Place — a tight cluster of historic homes, new builds, and a walkable retail strip with a residential mix of professors, students, and longtime homeowners. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served Rice Village families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-rice-village-houston-tx.localContent:
Rice Village is the small commercial-and-residential pocket centered on University Boulevard and Kirby Drive, immediately adjacent to Rice University and the city of West University Place. The residential streets that surround it — Sunset, Tangley, Bolsover, Bissonnet — hold a particular Houston housing pattern: pre-war and mid-century single-family homes, many sitting on lots that now command prices well above what the original owners paid for the entire property, alongside a steady stream of new-build townhomes and replacement custom homes. Many residents are Rice faculty, graduate students, medical professionals working at the nearby Texas Medical Center, and longtime homeowners whose grown children have left and whose home is now their largest single asset.

That demographic shapes our probate and estate planning work for Rice Village. We see longtime owners doing late-life planning around a home that has appreciated several times over, faculty households with TIAA retirement accounts and out-of-state property from prior academic posts, and adult children of recently deceased parents who need to administer estates in which the home is the central asset. The work is often less about complexity and more about matching the right tool — Muniment of Title, Transfer on Death Deed, or trust — to the specific facts.

Probate matters for Rice Village residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Rice Village Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Harris County:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — common for paid-down Rice Village homes where the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Rice Village Families

The right plan often centers on the home and the retirement accounts. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for faculty and professionals with out-of-state property
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Rice Village home out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for TIAA, 403(b), 401(k), and life insurance accounts

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Rice Village beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Rice Village and the Inner Loop

Our office serves clients throughout Rice Village, West University Place, Southside Place, the Museum District, the Texas Medical Center, Bellaire, and surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: We've lived on the same Rice Village street for 40 years. Will our home need probate when we die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on how it's titled and what other planning you've done. A home held jointly with right of survivorship, or covered by a recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed, transfers outside probate. A home held solely in one name without a TOD deed will need probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding. For longtime owners with a paid-down home, the Muniment of Title is often the simplest and least expensive path — usually 2 to 4 months in Harris County.

localFaqs.question: I'm a Rice University faculty member with TIAA retirement accounts. How do those pass at death?
localFaqs.answer: By beneficiary designation. TIAA, 403(b), 401(k), and IRAs all pass to whoever is named on the beneficiary form on file with the plan administrator — not by will. We routinely find that faculty clients have outdated beneficiaries, sometimes still listing an ex-spouse or a long-deceased parent. Reviewing and updating those designations is one of the simplest and most valuable pieces of planning we do.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Rice Village residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 sit at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a fifteen-minute drive from Rice Village. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at the time of filing.

localFaqs.question: Our parents recently died and left a Rice Village home. Where do we start?
localFaqs.answer: Start by gathering the will (if there is one), the deed, recent mortgage and property tax statements, and a basic asset list. From there, we can tell you whether a full administration is needed, whether a Muniment of Title will work, or whether the home was already structured to pass outside probate. The first consultation is free and we'll give you an honest assessment of how much legal work the estate actually requires.

probate-attorney-river-oaks-houston-tx.heroIntro: River Oaks is Houston's most established old-money neighborhood — historic mansions, multi-generational wealth, and the most sophisticated estate planning needs in the city. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served River Oaks families through Harris County probate, will contests, and high-value estate matters for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-river-oaks-houston-tx.localContent:
River Oaks was platted in the 1920s and remains the oldest established high-net-worth neighborhood in Houston. The community spans the streets between Buffalo Bayou and Westheimer, anchored by the River Oaks Country Club and the historic homes along Inverness, Lazy Lane, Del Monte, and Kirby. Many River Oaks families have lived here across two, three, or four generations, and the estate planning issues that come up reflect that depth: multi-generational trusts, family limited partnerships and family LLCs, charitable foundations and donor-advised funds, business succession for family-owned operating companies, and significant out-of-state and sometimes international holdings. The dollar amounts are larger than in most Houston neighborhoods, and the structures the family has already built are usually more elaborate.

Two issues come up repeatedly for River Oaks clients. The first is administration of an existing trust — making sure successor trustees actually understand their fiduciary duties, that distributions to beneficiaries are properly documented, and that the trust assets are properly retitled after a death. The second is contested probate or trust litigation, often within the family, when an aging matriarch or patriarch has recently signed a new will or amended a trust under circumstances that raise questions about capacity or undue influence. We handle both.

Probate matters for River Oaks residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free, confidential case review.

Probate Services for River Oaks Families

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for high-net-worth Inner Loop families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Trust administration — for successor trustees stepping into existing revocable or irrevocable trusts
  • Determination of Heirship — when needed to clear title or establish heirs
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Ancillary Probate — for River Oaks decedents with property in other states

Estate Planning for River Oaks Families

For most River Oaks clients, a will alone is not enough. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament — usually as the foundational document tied to a trust
  • Revocable Living Trusts and pour-over wills
  • Charitable bequests, charitable remainder trusts, and donor-advised fund coordination
  • Family LLCs and family limited partnership review
  • Durable Power of Attorney and Medical Power of Attorney
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds for residential property held outside the trust
  • Beneficiary designation coordination across retirement, brokerage, and life insurance accounts

Will Contests and Trust Litigation in Harris County

River Oaks generates more will-contest and trust-contest work than any other Houston neighborhood, and for understandable reasons — larger estates, more complex documents, and family dynamics that span generations. If you believe a parent's or grandparent's will or trust amendment was signed under undue influence, lack of capacity, or fraud, we represent contestants and proponents in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window matters; earlier action almost always strengthens the case. We also handle fiduciary breach claims against trustees and executors who are not fulfilling their duties.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for River Oaks beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

Serving River Oaks and the Inner Loop

Our office serves clients throughout River Oaks, Tanglewood, Memorial, Upper Kirby, and surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Our parents created a family trust years ago. When the surviving parent dies, do we still need probate?
localFaqs.answer: Often the answer is mostly no — if the trust was properly funded, meaning the assets were actually retitled into the trust during the parents' lifetimes, those assets pass under the trust without probate. But it is common for one or two assets to have been left out: a vehicle, a brokerage account, or a piece of real property purchased after the trust was signed. A short "clean-up" probate or Muniment of Title proceeding may be needed for those stragglers. We can review the asset list against the trust schedule and tell you exactly what's required.

localFaqs.question: I'm worried my elderly parent is being unduly influenced by a caregiver who has gotten a new will signed. What can I do?
localFaqs.answer: While the parent is still living, options include seeking a guardianship if capacity is genuinely impaired, or working through family channels to restore independent advisors. After death, the will can be contested in the Harris County Probate Courts on grounds of undue influence, lack of testamentary capacity, or fraud — but the two-year statutory window starts at the opening of probate, so prompt action matters. We handle these cases regularly and can advise on the strongest path forward based on the specific facts.

localFaqs.question: We have a family LLC holding investment real estate. How does that interact with probate?
localFaqs.answer: The LLC interest itself — your membership units — passes through your estate, by will or by trust, just like any other intangible asset. The underlying real estate stays inside the LLC and isn't directly affected. The key planning issue is the LLC's operating agreement: many family LLCs have transfer restrictions, buy-sell provisions, or successor manager language that controls what happens at a member's death. We coordinate with corporate counsel as needed to make sure the estate plan and the operating agreement actually fit together.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for River Oaks residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a ten-minute drive from most River Oaks addresses. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing.

probate-attorney-upper-kirby-houston-tx.heroIntro: The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has helped Upper Kirby families navigate Harris County probate and estate planning for over 25 years. Whether you're administering an estate, contesting a will, or putting a plan in place to protect your home and assets, we work with you directly — attorney to client.
probate-attorney-upper-kirby-houston-tx.localContent:
Upper Kirby sits in the heart of Houston's Inner Loop, just south of River Oaks and west of Greenway Plaza, anchored by the Kirby Drive corridor and the dining and shopping along West Alabama, Westheimer, and Richmond. It's a neighborhood of high-rise condos, mid-rise townhomes, and pocket single-family streets — and that mix shapes the kind of probate and estate planning issues we see for clients here. Many Upper Kirby residents own their primary home through an HOA or condominium association, hold investment property elsewhere in Texas, and have a mix of brokerage and retirement accounts. Each of those assets transfers differently at death, and getting them aligned in advance is one of the most useful things an estate plan can do.

Because Upper Kirby is in the City of Houston and Harris County, probate matters for residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned among the four courts, and each judge runs a slightly different docket. Knowing the local procedures of each court is part of how we keep uncontested probate moving efficiently for our Upper Kirby clients.

If you need to probate a will, open an heirship proceeding when there is no will, contest a will you believe was signed under undue influence, or put a plan in place that keeps your home and accounts out of court entirely, we'd like to help. Call us at (281) 609-0303 or request a free case review — we'll tell you honestly whether you need a lawyer at all.

Probate Services for Upper Kirby Residents

Probate is the court-supervised process of settling a deceased person's estate. We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for Inner Loop families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal court administration with or without an independent executor
  • Muniment of Title — a faster, lower-cost alternative when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage and only needs title transferred
  • Determination of Heirship — used when there is no valid will, to establish legal heirs under Texas intestate succession
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors and administrators on their legal duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold ($75,000 in non-real-property assets)

Estate Planning for Upper Kirby Families

A well-built estate plan keeps your family out of probate court whenever possible, minimizes delays, and ensures your wishes are honored if you can't speak for yourself. We help Upper Kirby residents put together plans that include:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful when you own property outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — transfer your home outside of probate, which is especially common among Inner Loop condo owners

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

If you believe a will was signed under undue influence, lack of testamentary capacity, or fraud, we represent contestants and proponents in will contests at the Harris County Probate Courts. We also represent beneficiaries who suspect a trustee or executor has breached fiduciary duty — failing to account, self-dealing, or misappropriating estate assets.

Life Insurance Disputes

Life insurance carriers sometimes deny or delay legitimate beneficiary claims, particularly when contestability period issues, beneficiary designation disputes, or interpleader actions arise. We pursue contested life insurance claims for Upper Kirby families and others throughout Southeast Texas — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Upper Kirby and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout the greater Houston area, including Upper Kirby, River Oaks, Greenway Plaza, Montrose, the Museum District, and all surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. When you work with us, you work directly with an attorney — not a paralegal or case manager — from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Which Harris County Probate Court will hear my case if I live in Upper Kirby?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County has four statutory probate courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — and your case is randomly assigned at the time of filing. All four sit at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a ten-minute drive from Upper Kirby. The case is heard by whichever court draws the assignment; you don't choose. We handle filings in all four courts regularly.

localFaqs.question: I own a condo in Upper Kirby — does it have to go through probate when I die?
localFaqs.answer: Not necessarily. If you hold the condo jointly with right of survivorship, or if you've recorded a Texas Transfer on Death Deed naming a beneficiary, title can pass outside of probate. If you hold it solely in your name with no TOD deed and no living trust, your beneficiaries will need probate (or a Muniment of Title proceeding if the estate has no unpaid debts other than the mortgage) to transfer title. We can review your deed and tell you exactly which path applies.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: For an uncontested independent administration in Harris County, expect roughly 4 to 8 months from filing to closing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court, complexity of the estate, and whether all heirs cooperate. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: I'm an out-of-state executor and the deceased lived in Upper Kirby. Do I need to come to Houston?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes for the initial probate hearing, but Harris County probate courts now allow many subsequent appearances by Zoom. We routinely represent out-of-state executors and can serve as the registered agent in Texas, handle filings, attend hearings, and coordinate with you remotely so your travel is limited to what's strictly necessary.

probate-attorney-greenway-plaza-houston-.heroIntro: Greenway Plaza is a dense, mixed-use district of office towers, high-rise condos, and townhomes wedged between Upper Kirby and West University. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served Greenway Plaza professionals through Harris County probate and estate planning for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-greenway-plaza-houston-.localContent:
Greenway Plaza sits along the U.S. 59 corridor between Buffalo Speedway and Edloe, anchored by the cluster of office towers that gave the district its name and ringed by mid-rise and high-rise condominiums and a growing inventory of townhomes. The neighborhood houses a particular Houston demographic — working professionals in finance, energy, law, and consulting, many of them young or mid-career, often without children, frequently with significant compensation in equity grants, deferred comp, and 401(k) balances rather than in real property. The asset profile is closer to Upper Kirby's than to River Oaks's: the home or condo is real but is rarely the largest asset on the balance sheet.

That asset profile shapes our planning work for Greenway Plaza clients. The biggest single piece of value is usually in retirement accounts and equity compensation, both of which pass by beneficiary designation rather than by will. The home or condo benefits most from a Transfer on Death Deed or revocable trust to keep it out of probate. And out-of-state ties — family in another part of the country, a vacation property somewhere, or a prior home that hasn't sold — often need a coordinated plan to avoid an unnecessary second probate.

Probate matters for Greenway Plaza residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Greenway Plaza Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Harris County:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — for estates with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Greenway Plaza Professionals

The high-value moves for most Greenway Plaza professionals are simpler than people assume. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful for clients with property outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Greenway Plaza condo or townhome out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for 401(k), brokerage, RSU, and life insurance accounts

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Serving Greenway Plaza and the Inner Loop

Our office serves clients throughout Greenway Plaza, Upper Kirby, West University Place, the Museum District, Montrose, and surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: I work in finance and most of my net worth is in my 401(k) and a brokerage account. Will those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no, if beneficiaries are properly named. 401(k)s, IRAs, and brokerage accounts with a Transfer on Death (TOD) registration pass directly to the named beneficiary outside probate. Brokerage accounts without a TOD registration, and any account where the beneficiary form was never completed or is outdated, do go through probate. Reviewing the designations once a year is the single highest-value piece of estate planning we do for Greenway Plaza professionals.

localFaqs.question: I have unvested RSUs at my employer. What happens to those if I die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the equity plan document. Many plans accelerate vesting at death and pay the value to the named beneficiary or to the estate; others terminate unvested grants entirely. Vested-but-unexercised options usually remain exercisable for a limited window after death by the executor or beneficiary. We review equity grant agreements as part of estate planning so the answer isn't a surprise to your family.

localFaqs.question: I own a condo at Greenway Plaza and a vacation home in Colorado. Two probates?
localFaqs.answer: Without planning, yes. Real property in another state, titled in your individual name, typically triggers a separate "ancillary probate" in that state — meaning your family runs two probates. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that. We coordinate with Colorado counsel as needed.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Greenway Plaza residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a ten-minute drive up Spur 527 from Greenway Plaza. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing.

probate-attorney-third-ward-houston-tx.heroIntro: The Third Ward — historic home of Texas Southern University and one of Houston's most established Black communities — has deep family roots, longtime homeownership, and a recurring need for probate work to keep family homes in the family. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served Third Ward families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-third-ward-houston-tx.localContent:
The Third Ward stretches southeast of Downtown, anchored by Texas Southern University, the University of Houston main campus, MacGregor Park, and the historic commercial corridor along Dowling and Almeda. The neighborhood is one of Houston's oldest and most established Black communities, with families who have owned homes here across two, three, or even four generations. That longevity is the neighborhood's strength — and it also produces a recurring legal issue we help with frequently. When a homeowner dies and probate is never opened, the deed remains in the deceased's name. Decades later, when the family wants to refinance, sell, or simply pass the home cleanly to the next generation, title cannot move because no court order has ever established who the legal heirs are.

The fix in those cases is a Determination of Heirship — a Harris County Probate Court proceeding that establishes the legal heirs of a deceased property owner under Texas intestate succession law. Where two or three generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one for each generation. The work is solvable, often less expensive than people assume, and it is one of the most important things a family can do to protect a home that has been in the family for a long time. We handle these cases respectfully and practically for Third Ward families on a regular basis.

Probate matters for Third Ward residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Third Ward Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Harris County, with particular experience in heirship and family-home title work:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Determination of Heirship — for family homes where the original owner died without probate, often years or decades ago
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for estates with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Affidavit of Heirship — sometimes a simpler tool, where appropriate, to record family history for title purposes

Estate Planning to Keep the Family Home in the Family

The most useful estate planning we do for Third Ward families is often the simplest. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — a recordable, low-cost tool that names a beneficiary for the home and avoids probate entirely
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Revocable Living Trusts, where appropriate for larger or more complex estates

A recorded Transfer on Death Deed is often the single most valuable document a Third Ward homeowner can put in place. It is recordable, revocable, costs little to prepare, and lets the home pass to the named beneficiary without probate at all.

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Third Ward families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving the Third Ward and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout the Third Ward, MacGregor, Riverside Terrace, the East End, EaDo, and surrounding neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Our family home in the Third Ward is still in my grandfather's name and he passed away in the 1990s. Can we still fix it?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be cleared through a Determination of Heirship in the Harris County Probate Court. Where multiple generations have passed without probate, a series of heirship proceedings may be needed — one for each generation — but the result is a clear chain of title that finally lets the family refinance, sell, or pass the home to the next generation cleanly. The work is solvable and often less expensive than families expect.

localFaqs.question: What's the difference between an Affidavit of Heirship and a Determination of Heirship?
localFaqs.answer: An Affidavit of Heirship is a sworn statement, usually by two non-family witnesses who knew the family, recorded in the county property records to establish family history. It is cheaper but not always sufficient for a title company or buyer — many will accept it for a sale only after it has been on record for several years. A Determination of Heirship is a court order from the Harris County Probate Court that conclusively establishes the heirs and clears title immediately. The right tool depends on what you need to do next with the property; we can advise on which path fits.

localFaqs.question: I own a Third Ward home and want to leave it to my children without sending them through probate. How?
localFaqs.answer: The simplest tool is a Texas Transfer on Death Deed — a recordable instrument that names beneficiaries for the home and transfers title outside probate when you die. It is revocable during your lifetime, costs little to prepare and record, and avoids the need for any court proceeding at your death. For most Third Ward homeowners, a TOD deed plus a will is the most practical, lowest-cost plan.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Third Ward residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston — about a ten-minute drive from the Third Ward. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing.

probate-attorney-fourth-ward-houston-tx.heroIntro: Fourth Ward — Freedmen's Town — is the oldest free Black community in Texas, with brick streets laid by the freedmen themselves and family lineages that stretch back to the 1860s. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has served Fourth Ward families through Harris County probate and Determinations of Heirship for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-fourth-ward-houston-tx.localContent:
Fourth Ward, also known as Freedmen's Town, lies just west of Downtown Houston and was founded in the years immediately after Emancipation by formerly enslaved people who built the neighborhood by hand — including the brick streets that still survive in parts of the historic district. Generations of families have owned homes here, and the neighborhood holds extraordinary cultural and historical significance. Today the community sits at the crossroads of preservation and rapid redevelopment, with new mid-rise townhomes pressing up against the historic shotgun houses, churches, and family-owned lots that have been in some families for over a century.

That history produces a recurring legal need we help with frequently: family-owned property where the original owner of record died long ago, probate was never opened, and the deed has remained in the deceased's name across one or more generations. When a family is approached by a developer, decides to refinance, or simply wants to pass the property to the next generation cleanly, title cannot move until the chain is cleared. The fix is a Determination of Heirship in the Harris County Probate Court — sometimes a series of them, one for each generation that died without probate. The work takes care, but it is solvable, and it is often the single most important thing a family can do to protect a property that has been in the family for generations.

Probate matters for Fourth Ward residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown, less than a mile from the heart of Freedmen's Town. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free, no-pressure case review.

Probate Services for Fourth Ward Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Harris County, with particular experience in heirship and historic-property title work:

  • Determination of Heirship — for family-owned property where the chain of title was never cleared after a death, sometimes generations ago
  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage

localFaqs.question: Our family has owned a Freedmen's Town home since the 1920s, but the deed is still in my great-grandmother's name. Can we still establish title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Long-held family property where successive generations died without probate can be cleared through Determinations of Heirship in the Harris County Probate Court — sometimes one proceeding per generation that didn't go through probate. The work is more involved than a routine administration, but it is solvable, and the resulting court orders create a clean, recordable chain of title that finally lets the family use, sell, or pass the property as it sees fit.

localFaqs.question: Developers keep approaching us about our Fourth Ward property. Should we clear title first before deciding anything?
localFaqs.answer: Usually yes. Without clear title, the family can't sell — and can't even make an informed decision about whether to sell — because no buyer or title company will close on property whose chain of title has gaps. Clearing title through heirship proceedings puts the family in the strongest position to consider any offer on its own terms, including the option to keep the property and pass it to the next generation. We can walk through the process and the cost in a free consultation.

localFaqs.question: How can I make sure my home passes to my children without putting them through probate?
localFaqs.answer: A recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed is usually the simplest, least expensive tool. It names beneficiaries for the home and transfers title outside probate at your death; it is revocable during your lifetime; and it does not require any court proceeding at your death. Combined with a basic will, it covers most Fourth Ward homeowners' needs at a fraction of the cost of a trust.

The deed remains in the deceased's name. localFaqs.question: Our family home in the Fifth Ward has been in the family since the 1950s and the deed still has my grandparents' names on it. Is that a problem?
localFaqs.answer: It can be when the family wants to do anything that requires clear title — refinancing, selling, or passing the home to the next generation through a clean recorded deed. The fix is a Determination of Heirship in the Harris County Probate Court, which establishes the legal heirs of the deceased owners under Texas law. Where two or three generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed. The work is solvable, and it is one of the most important things a Fifth Ward family can do to protect its property.

localFaqs.question: Several siblings inherited my parents' Fifth Ward home and we don't all agree on what to do with it. What are our options?
localFaqs.answer: If the heirs cannot agree, Texas law allows any co-owner to file a partition suit asking the court to divide the property — usually by sale, when the property cannot be physically split. Before going that route, however, it is often worth trying a buy-out among the siblings or a written family agreement that gives one sibling the right to live in the home in exchange for paying the others over time. We can advise on the options and represent any of the heirs in negotiation or, if needed, in court.

localFaqs.question: How can I make sure my home passes to my children without sending them through probate?
localFaqs.answer: A recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed is usually the simplest tool. It names beneficiaries, takes effect at your death, is revocable during your lifetime, and avoids the need for probate on the home entirely. For homeowners whose primary asset is the home itself, a TOD deed plus a basic will is often the most practical and affordable plan available.

localFaqs.question: Our family home in the East End has been in the family for three generations and the deed is still in my grandfather's name. Can we clear it up?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Long-held family property where successive generations died without probate can be cleared through Determinations of Heirship in the Harris County Probate Court. Where multiple generations have died without probate, a series of heirship proceedings may be needed — one per generation — but the result is a clear, recordable chain of title. localFaqs.question: Three generations live in our East End home. How do we make sure everyone is protected if my parents pass?
localFaqs.answer: The starting point is to understand whose name is on the deed today and what each family member would want to happen at each parent's death. From there, the right combination usually includes a will, a Transfer on Death Deed naming beneficiaries, and possibly a written family agreement covering things like who has the right to live in the home and how household expenses are shared. We work through these conversations with East End families regularly and tailor the plan to the family's actual circumstances.

localFaqs.question: What's the difference between an Affidavit of Heirship and a Determination of Heirship?
localFaqs.answer: An Affidavit of Heirship is a sworn statement, usually by two non-family witnesses, recorded in the county property records. It is cheaper but not always sufficient on its own to satisfy a title company or buyer — many will only accept it after it has been on record for several years. A Determination of Heirship is a court order from the Harris County Probate Court that conclusively establishes the heirs and immediately clears title. The right tool depends on what you need to do next with the property.

localFaqs.question: I retired to Lake Conroe from another state. Do I need a new will?
localFaqs.answer: Your existing will is likely valid in Texas if it was validly executed under your prior state's law, but Texas has unique rules — the option of independent administration, community property treatment of marital assets, the Transfer on Death Deed — that an older out-of-state will rarely takes advantage of. We typically recommend a Texas-specific will or trust update once you've established residency here.

localFaqs.question: I retired to Lake Conroe from another state — is my old will still valid in Texas?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes, if it was validly executed under your prior state's law it will be recognized in Texas. But Texas has features that an out-of-state will rarely takes advantage of — independent administration, community property treatment of marital assets, the Transfer on Death Deed, and the Muniment of Title shortcut. We typically recommend a Texas-specific will or trust update once you've established residency in Willis.

localFaqs.question: I own a lakefront home on Lake Conroe and a condo in Florida. What does that mean at death?
localFaqs.answer: Real property in another state typically triggers a separate ancillary probate in that state if it's titled in your individual name — meaning your family runs two probates instead of one. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that. We can review your deeds and tell you whether a trust makes sense for your specific situation.

localFaqs.question: My adult kids live out of state and one is the executor. Will they have to come to Texas?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes for the initial probate hearing, but Montgomery County Probate Court allows many subsequent appearances by Zoom. We routinely represent out-of-state executors and can serve as the registered agent in Texas, handle filings, attend hearings, and coordinate with your child remotely so their travel is limited to what's strictly necessary.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Montgomery County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1 generally close in 4 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — if the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage.

probate-attorney-kingwood-tx.heroIntro: Kingwood — the "Livable Forest" — was annexed by the City of Houston in 1996 but remains entirely in Harris County for probate purposes. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has guided Kingwood families through Harris County probate for over 25 years, with particular experience serving its established and aging population.
probate-attorney-kingwood-tx.localContent:
Kingwood has a character all its own. Built starting in the 1970s as a master-planned community of "villages" wrapped in pine forest along the West Fork of the San Jacinto, it was annexed by the City of Houston in 1996 — a contentious history that still flavors how some long-time residents see their relationship with city government. For probate purposes, none of that annexation history matters: Kingwood was, and is, in Harris County, so probate cases for Kingwood residents are heard at the Harris County Probate Courts in downtown Houston. What does matter is the demographic that came with five decades of residence in the same community. Kingwood has a substantial population of original homeowners now in their 70s and 80s, with grown children scattered across the country, paid-off homes that have appreciated significantly, and retirement accounts that built up over long careers in the energy industry. That's a particular probate and estate planning profile, and it shapes the kind of work we do here.

Probate matters for Kingwood residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Even though Kingwood feels like its own community out among the pines, all four courts handle Kingwood cases and we file in each of them regularly.

If you're administering a parent's estate, dealing with a contested will, or putting a plan in place that protects a Kingwood home and accounts, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Kingwood homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; particularly common for paid-off original Kingwood homes
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful when adult children live out of state, or for blended families
  • Durable Power of Attorney — particularly important as residents age
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Kingwood home out of probate
