Will contests involving elderly Kingwood residents are something we handle with regularity. If you believe a parent's will was signed under undue influence — a sibling, caregiver, or new spouse exerting pressure — we represent contestants and proponents in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

localFaqs.question: Kingwood was annexed by Houston in 1996. Does that mean the City of Houston handles probate?
localFaqs.answer: No. Cities don't run probate courts in Texas — counties do. Kingwood is and has always been in Harris County, so probate matters for Kingwood residents are heard by the Harris County Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street, regardless of the annexation. The annexation affects city services and taxes, not probate jurisdiction.

localFaqs.question: My parents have lived in their original Kingwood home since the 1970s and it's paid off. Is full probate necessary?
localFaqs.answer: Often not. If the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage — and a paid-off home is a big advantage — a Muniment of Title proceeding can transfer the home with a single court hearing rather than a full administration. It typically closes in 2 to 4 months and saves substantial cost. We can review the estate and tell you whether it qualifies.

localFaqs.question: Mom is showing signs of dementia. Can she still sign a will or power of attorney?
localFaqs.answer: Possibly — Texas requires testamentary capacity at the moment of signing, not perfect cognition all the time. Someone in early or fluctuating dementia may still have capacity on a good day. The risk is that any will or power of attorney signed during decline becomes a target for a contest later. We handle these situations carefully, often with medical documentation contemporaneous to signing, and sometimes recommend earlier action when capacity is clearer.

localFaqs.question: My adult kids live in Colorado and California. How do I make this easy for them?
localFaqs.answer: The single highest-impact step is putting your home into a Transfer on Death Deed and confirming beneficiary designations on every retirement account, brokerage account, and life insurance policy. Done well, those steps can move 80 to 90 percent of a typical Kingwood estate outside of probate entirely — meaning your kids handle paperwork, not court hearings. A simple will catches what's left.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Humble residents?
localFaqs.answer: Humble is in Harris County, so probate cases are filed with the Harris County Clerk and heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing.

localFaqs.question: My parents lived in Humble for fifty years and the home title still shows my grandparents' names. Can we fix that?
localFaqs.answer: Yes. When a homeowner dies and the estate is never probated, the title stays in the deceased's name even though the family continued living there. The fix is a Determination of Heirship — sometimes a series of them, one per generation — that legally establishes who owns the property today. We handle these regularly for older Humble families.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — often 2 to 4 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: I just bought my first home in Humble. Do I really need a will at 30?
localFaqs.answer: You don't strictly need one — Texas intestate succession will distribute your assets if you die without a will. But intestate distribution rarely matches what most people would actually want, especially if you're unmarried, have a partner you're not married to, or have minor children. A simple will and Transfer on Death Deed for the house typically cost a fraction of what an unprobated mess costs to clean up later.

localFaqs.question: We have two kids under 10. Who should we name as guardian in our will?
localFaqs.answer: Pick someone who shares your values, is financially stable enough to take on the responsibility, and is geographically practical for the kids. A common mistake is naming the same person as both guardian (of the children) and trustee (of the money) — sometimes the right caregiver isn't the right financial manager. We help Atascocita parents work through both designations and structure a testamentary trust that releases funds to the kids over time rather than in a single lump sum at 18.

localFaqs.question: I named my 8-year-old as the beneficiary on my life insurance. Is that a problem?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, often. A life insurance company can't pay benefits directly to a minor — the money goes into a court-supervised guardianship of the estate, which is expensive, slow, and ends with the child receiving everything outright at 18. Naming a properly drafted trust as beneficiary instead lets the funds be managed for the child by someone you choose, with distributions on the timeline you choose. This is one of the most common and most fixable mistakes we see in young family estate plans.

localFaqs.question: Porter is right on the Harris/Montgomery line. How do I know which county handles probate?
localFaqs.answer: Probate jurisdiction follows the deceased's legal residence at the time of death — confirmed by checking the actual property records, not the ZIP code or mailing address. Most of Porter is firmly in Montgomery County, but homes south of FM 1485 and along the Harris County line can sometimes sit in Harris County. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation before any filing.

localFaqs.question: We have 15 acres in Porter that's been in the family for two generations. Can we put it into a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, and for many landowning families that's the right move. A revocable living trust holding the acreage avoids probate entirely at death, lets you specify how the land should be divided or kept whole among heirs, and can include conditions if the operation needs to continue. We can sit down and walk through whether a trust makes sense for your specific land and family.

localFaqs.question: Our family has owned land off Highway 242 since the 1960s. We never probated my grandfather's will. Is that a problem?

localFaqs.question: We just moved into Valley Ranch. What's the simplest first estate planning step?

localFaqs.question: I own a closely held business in Shenandoah. What happens to it when I die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on three things: whether you have a will, whether the business has a buy-sell agreement with any partners, and how the ownership interest is titled. Without planning, the business interest passes through probate and the executor (often a spouse who has no involvement in operations) ends up making decisions in real time. A revocable living trust holding the interest, paired with a buy-sell agreement among partners, generally avoids that. We frequently coordinate the two for Shenandoah business owners.

localFaqs.question: I have RSUs and deferred compensation from an employer in The Woodlands. How does that pass at death?
localFaqs.answer: Vested stock that's been delivered to a brokerage account passes by beneficiary designation if one is named, otherwise through probate. Unvested RSUs, deferred comp, and unexercised options are usually governed by the plan document — many plans accelerate vesting at death and pay to the named beneficiary, but plan terms vary widely. We review equity and deferred comp grant agreements as part of estate planning for Shenandoah-area executives.

localFaqs.question: My sibling moved in to care for Mom in her last two years. We just learned the will was changed during that time to leave the house to my sibling. Do I have a case?
localFaqs.answer: Possibly — this is a classic undue-influence fact pattern, and Texas has a body of law specifically addressing it. The contest needs to show that the influence overcame the testator's free will, often through evidence of isolation, control over communications, control over medical or financial decisions, and a sudden change in disposition. The two-year statutory window to contest starts at the opening of probate, so timing matters. We'd want to review the medical records, prior wills, and the circumstances of the change in detail.

localFaqs.answer: The FM 1960 corridor itself runs entirely through Harris County. However, communities just north of FM 1960 — Spring proper, parts of the Klein area, and points further north — can sit in Montgomery County. localFaqs.question: I have homes in two different counties — does probate happen twice?
localFaqs.answer: Generally, primary probate happens in one county — the county of your legal residence. Real property in another Texas county is typically handled within that same probate by recording certified copies of the probate documents in the second county's deed records. A separate "ancillary" probate is required when the second property is in a different state. A revocable living trust holding the secondary property usually avoids that.

localFaqs.question: We have two young kids in Bridgeland. What's the most important first estate planning step?
localFaqs.answer: Naming a guardian for the children in your will is usually the first and most important step — it's the one decision a court genuinely cannot make as well as you can. The second is making sure life insurance and retirement accounts don't list a minor child as direct beneficiary, which would force a court-supervised guardianship of the estate. Naming a properly drafted trust as beneficiary instead solves both problems and lets you control how funds are released over time.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Bridgeland residents?
localFaqs.answer: Bridgeland is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing.

localFaqs.question: I named my 6-year-old as beneficiary on my life insurance. Should I change that?
localFaqs.answer: Yes. Insurance companies can't pay benefits directly to a minor — the funds go into a court-supervised guardianship that's expensive, slow, and ends with the child receiving everything outright at 18. Naming a properly drafted trust as beneficiary instead lets the funds be managed for the child by someone you choose, with distributions on the timeline you choose. This is one of the most common and most fixable mistakes we see in young family plans.

localFaqs.question: Mom and Dad paid off their Fairfield home and split most of their savings into IRAs. What actually has to go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Often less than people expect. IRAs and 401(k)s pass by beneficiary designation outside of probate, life insurance does the same, and a paid-off home with a recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed transfers without probate too. What's left for probate is usually any account without a beneficiary designation, vehicles, and personal property. In well-planned Fairfield estates, the actual probate is often quite limited — sometimes a streamlined Muniment of Title is all that's needed.

localFaqs.question: We're empty nesters in Fairfield. Do we still need a will if we have a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes, even with a fully funded revocable living trust. A "pour-over" will is the safety net — it directs anything left outside the trust at death (a forgotten account, a vehicle, the contents of the house) into the trust. It also names guardians if there are still minor dependents and confirms wishes that the trust alone might not cover. Most well-built plans include both.

Copperfield is one of the older master-planned communities in northwest Harris County, with development beginning in the 1980s. The community sits along Highway 6 in the Cy-Fair area and includes a series of "villages" — Copperfield Westcreek Village, Middlegate Village, Northmead Village, Southcreek Village, and others — each with its own character but tied together by mature trees, established schools, and a population that's aged in place over the last three or four decades. Many Copperfield residents bought their homes new, raised their kids there, and are now navigating the late-career or early-retirement stage with adult children who've moved on. That's a particular probate and estate planning profile, and it shapes the kind of work we do here.

Copperfield is in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing, and uncontested administrations generally close in 4 to 8 months.

Whether you're administering a parent's estate, dealing with a contested will, or putting a plan in place that simplifies things for your kids, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Copperfield Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in the Harris County Probate Courts:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Copperfield homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for paid-off original homes
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Copperfield Families

A clear plan keeps your home and accounts out of court whenever possible. We help Copperfield residents with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful when adult children live out of state or for blended families
  • Durable Power of Attorney — particularly important as residents age
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Copperfield home out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for retirement accounts and life insurance

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

Will contests involving elderly parents and adult-sibling disputes are something we handle regularly. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Copperfield residents?
localFaqs.answer: Copperfield is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing.

localFaqs.question: Mom has lived in her Copperfield home since 1985 and the mortgage was paid off years ago. Is full probate necessary?
localFaqs.answer: Often not. With a paid-off home and no other unpaid debts, the estate likely qualifies for a Muniment of Title proceeding — a streamlined court process that transfers the home with a single hearing rather than a full administration. It typically closes in 2 to 4 months in Harris County. We see this fact pattern often in Copperfield and can confirm whether the estate qualifies.

localFaqs.question: I'm Mom's only child but she remarried late in life. Now her stepchildren are claiming half the house. Do they have a claim?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the will, the deed, and the marital property history. Under Texas community property law, a surviving spouse generally has rights to community property; a step-spouse acquired late in life may also have homestead rights. Whether stepchildren inherit anything depends entirely on the will's language. These cases come up often in Copperfield, and the right answer requires reviewing the actual documents — guesses based on family dynamics alone tend to be wrong.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to.

localFaqs.question: Our home has a private boat dock and a waterway easement. Does that complicate probate?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no — easements and HOA-attached privileges typically run with the land and pass with the home. But it's worth confirming that any separate dock structure, lift, or waterway agreement is properly documented in the deed records, because anything titled separately needs separate handling. We can review the deed and HOA documents as part of estate planning to make sure the waterfront features pass cleanly.

localFaqs.question: I have RSUs from my employer that haven't vested yet. What happens to them if I die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the equity plan. Many employer plans accelerate vesting at death and pay to a named beneficiary — but the terms vary widely, and some plans forfeit unvested grants entirely. We review equity grant agreements as part of estate planning for Towne Lake professionals so you understand exactly what would happen and can plan around it.

localFaqs.question: I own a townhome in the Vintage Park area. Does it have to go through probate when I die?
localFaqs.answer: Not necessarily. If the townhome is held jointly with right of survivorship, or if you've recorded a Texas Transfer on Death Deed naming a beneficiary, title can pass outside of probate. If it's held solely in your name with no TOD deed and no living trust, your beneficiaries will need probate (or a Muniment of Title proceeding if the estate has no unpaid debts other than the mortgage) to transfer title. We can review your deed and tell you exactly which path applies.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Vintage Park residents?
localFaqs.answer: The Vintage Park area is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston.

localFaqs.question: My condo is held in an LLC for tax reasons. How does that affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: The condo itself isn't probated — what passes through your estate is your interest in the LLC. The LLC's operating agreement controls what happens to that interest at death, and many operating agreements have specific transfer-on-death or buyout provisions. We frequently coordinate the LLC operating agreement with the rest of the estate plan to make sure the structure works as intended.

localFaqs.question: Hockley is on the Harris/Waller County line. How do I know which county handles probate?
localFaqs.answer: Probate jurisdiction follows the deceased's actual legal residence, confirmed by checking the property records — not the ZIP code, mailing address, or which side of the road the driveway is on. Most of Hockley sits in Harris County, but properties along the western edge can fall in Waller County. We confirm the correct county at the first consultation before any filing.

localFaqs.question: Our family has owned 60 acres in Hockley for three generations. Probate was never done. Can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is required to walk the chain forward. It takes more work than a routine administration, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for northwest Harris and Waller County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: We have a working horse property with livestock and equipment. Should we put it in a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes — particularly when more than one heir will inherit, or when the operation needs to continue uninterrupted after a death. A revocable living trust holding the land, equipment, and livestock can avoid probate entirely and keep the operation running through the transition. We can sit down with you and walk through whether a trust makes sense for your specific land and family circumstances.

probate-attorney-energy-corridor-houston.heroIntro: The Energy Corridor along I-10 west is home to BP, Shell, ConocoPhillips, and dozens of other operators — and to thousands of energy professionals with stock options, RSUs, and overseas assignments to plan around. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has guided Energy Corridor families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-energy-corridor-houston.localContent:
The Energy Corridor stretches along Interstate 10 between the Beltway and the Grand Parkway, anchored by the corporate campuses of BP America, Shell, ConocoPhillips, Wood, Worley, and a dense cluster of oilfield services and engineering firms. The professional community here is unusual in one important respect — a meaningful share of Energy Corridor residents have lived and worked overseas on expat assignments, and many still hold foreign-titled property, foreign retirement accounts, or compensation paid through non-U.S. entities. Those facts dramatically change what an estate plan needs to look like, and they're a recurring theme in the planning work we do for Energy Corridor families.

The other distinguishing feature of the area is layered employer compensation: vested and unvested stock, deferred compensation, performance shares, supplemental executive retirement plans, and 401(k)s alongside the traditional pension plans some legacy employers still offer. Each of those instruments transfers differently at death — some by beneficiary designation, some by plan document, some through probate — and getting them aligned is one of the highest-value things an estate plan can do for an industry professional. We see avoidable mistakes here regularly: outdated beneficiary forms naming an ex-spouse, RSU grants that defaulted to the estate because no beneficiary was filed, equity that ended up in probate court when it didn't have to.

Energy Corridor sits in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — with cases randomly assigned at filing. If you're administering an estate, navigating multi-country assets, or putting a plan in place that ties your global compensation together, call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: I worked overseas on an expat assignment and still have a UK pension and a flat in London. How does that affect my Texas estate plan?
localFaqs.answer: Significantly. Texas-titled assets pass through Texas probate; UK-titled real estate and pensions are governed by UK law and typically require a separate UK probate ("grant of probate") regardless of what your Texas will says. A coordinated plan often uses a Texas will plus a UK-side instrument, and sometimes a revocable trust holding the Texas assets. We work with Energy Corridor clients on multi-country planning regularly and coordinate with foreign counsel where needed.

localFaqs.question: Which Harris County Probate Court will hear my case if I live in the Energy Corridor?
localFaqs.answer: Cases are randomly assigned among Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 at the time of filing — all four sit at 201 Caroline Street downtown, about a 25-minute drive east of the Energy Corridor. Each judge runs a slightly different docket, and we file in all four regularly.

localFaqs.question: I have RSUs and unvested stock options through my employer. Do they go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the equity plan. Vested stock that's been transferred to a brokerage account passes by beneficiary designation if one is named, otherwise through probate. Unvested RSUs and unexercised options are governed by the plan document — many plans accelerate vesting at death and pay to a named beneficiary, but plans differ. We review equity grant agreements as part of estate planning for Energy Corridor industry clients.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: For an uncontested independent administration, expect roughly 4 to 8 months from filing to closing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court, complexity of the estate, and whether all heirs cooperate. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months.

probate-attorney-memorial-houston-tx.heroIntro: Memorial is one of Houston's most established affluent neighborhoods — large lots, mature pines, and many families who have lived along Memorial Drive for two or three generations. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Harris County probate and estate planning for Memorial families for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-memorial-houston-tx.localContent:
Memorial covers a long stretch of West Houston roughly framed by Memorial Drive, the Sam Houston Tollway, Buffalo Bayou, and the Memorial Villages to the north. The neighborhood character is shaped by mature pine and oak canopy, large single-family lots, and a pattern of multi-generational ownership that's becoming uncommon in Houston — it's not unusual to meet Memorial residents whose grandparents or parents bought the family home in the 1960s or 70s. That ownership pattern produces a recurring set of probate questions: a parent passes, the home is paid off, the children want to keep it (or sell it), and no one ever cleared the chain of title from the prior generation.

Many Memorial homes have also appreciated dramatically. A house that originally cost $50,000 may now be worth well over a million, and that step-up in basis at death is one of the most useful — and most underused — features of the federal estate tax code. Coordinating a will or trust with the basis rules can save heirs significant capital gains tax if and when the home is eventually sold. We talk through this with Memorial clients regularly during planning.

Memorial sits in Harris County (with the Memorial Villages — Piney Point, Hunters Creek, Bunker Hill, and the others — as separately incorporated cities embedded inside it). All probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street, with cases randomly assigned at filing. Whether you're administering a parent's estate, contesting a will, or planning the transfer of a long-held family home, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: My parents bought their Memorial home in 1972 and never set up a trust. What do we do when they pass?
localFaqs.answer: If the home is in their individual names with no Transfer on Death Deed and no living trust, it has to pass through Texas probate or by Muniment of Title. If the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage (and many long-held Memorial homes are paid off entirely), Muniment of Title is often the most efficient route — sometimes closing in 2 to 4 months. We can review the deed and tell you exactly which option applies.

localFaqs.question: How does the step-up in basis affect a Memorial home that has appreciated significantly?
localFaqs.answer: When a homeowner dies, the property's tax basis is generally stepped up to fair market value at the date of death — meaning if the heirs sell shortly afterward, capital gains tax is minimal or zero. This is one of the most valuable features of the federal tax code for inherited homes, and it survives in Texas regardless of whether the home passes through probate, by trust, or by Transfer on Death Deed. It's why we generally don't recommend transferring a Memorial home to your children during your lifetime — they would lose the step-up.

localFaqs.question: Which probate court hears Memorial cases?
localFaqs.answer: Memorial is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing.

localFaqs.question: Multiple generations of my family have died without probate being opened. Can we still clear title to the house?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through one or more Determinations of Heirship — sometimes a series of them, working back through each generation. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for Memorial families regularly.

probate-attorney-memorial-city-houston-t.heroIntro: Memorial City is the medical, retail, and residential hub at the heart of West Houston — a dense mix of high-rise condos, townhomes, and the campuses around Memorial City Mall and the Memorial Hermann medical complex. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Harris County probate for Memorial City residents for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-memorial-city-houston-t.localContent:
Memorial City has transformed in the last two decades from a 1960s-era shopping mall surrounded by ranch homes into a true urban node. The Memorial Hermann hospital campus, the rebuilt Memorial City Mall, dense high-rise condominiums along Gessner and Bunker Hill, and a steady wave of new townhome construction have changed the resident profile entirely. Today the area is home to medical professionals working at the hospital, retirees who downsized from larger Memorial-area homes into condos, and younger professionals choosing townhomes for the location and the schools. Each of those groups brings a different set of probate and estate planning needs.

One issue we see regularly for Memorial City residents involves high-rise condo title. Many condos were purchased in joint name with an adult child, in the hope that title would pass automatically — but joint tenancy in Texas does not automatically include right of survivorship the way it does in some other states. Without specific "with right of survivorship" language in the deed, the deceased owner's interest must still pass through probate. A quick deed review during planning can fix this for almost no cost.

Memorial City sits in Harris County. All probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street, with cases randomly assigned at filing. localFaqs.question: I added my daughter to the deed of my Memorial City condo years ago. Will she automatically own it when I die?
localFaqs.answer: Probably not — at least not without further action. Texas does not presume right of survivorship when two people are listed on a deed. Unless your deed contains specific "with right of survivorship" or "JTWROS" language, your half interest passes through your estate, not directly to your daughter. We review deeds for Memorial City clients regularly and can help correct survivorship language while you're still living.

localFaqs.question: Where do I file probate for a Memorial City resident?
localFaqs.answer: Memorial City is in Harris County, so probate is filed at the Harris County Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned to one of four courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at filing.

localFaqs.question: Memorial City has lots of HOAs and condo associations. Do they affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: HOAs don't change the probate procedure itself, but assessments continue to accrue during administration, and most condo associations require formal notice of the owner's death and proof of executor authority before they'll communicate with the family. We help Memorial City executors navigate that notice process and protect against late fees or liens.

localFaqs.question: I just downsized from a Memorial home into a Memorial City condo. Do I need to redo my will?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes — at least a review. A move from a single-family home to a condo can change beneficiary planning meaningfully (the family home is the asset most adult children expect to inherit). At a minimum we'd recommend updating the property description, considering a Texas Transfer on Death Deed for the new condo, and reviewing whether your existing will still reflects what you intend.

probate-attorney-spring-branch-houston-t.localContent:
Spring Branch is a wide swath of West Houston between I-10 and the Northwest Freeway, framed loosely by Beltway 8 to the west and Antoine Drive to the east. The neighborhood character is genuinely mixed — quiet 1950s and 60s bungalows on tree-lined streets, postwar ranch homes that sat for decades and are now being torn down for $700,000 new builds, the long-established Koreatown corridor along Long Point with its restaurants, churches, and community organizations, and a strong Latino population whose presence shapes much of the commercial life of the neighborhood. Spring Branch ISD anchors the family side of things and is one of the recurring reasons younger families move in.

That diversity translates directly into the kind of probate and estate planning work we do here. We see immigrant families where the parents own the home but the deed names only the husband, and the question becomes how to transfer to the surviving spouse and children efficiently. We see longtime owners whose gentrification-era home values now exceed what they expected, often without a current will. We see blended families and second marriages where the planning needs to balance the new spouse with adult children from a prior marriage. And we see clients whose primary documents were drafted in another country or another language — that's not necessarily a problem, but it usually warrants a Texas-specific update.

Spring Branch sits in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — with cases randomly assigned at filing. localFaqs.question: My parents' will is written in Korean. Can it be probated in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes, with a certified translation. Texas courts will accept a foreign-language will if it was validly executed under the law of the place where it was signed and a certified English translation is filed with the application. We routinely work with Spring Branch families to probate wills originally drafted abroad. That said, a Texas-specific will usually streamlines administration significantly, and we'll talk through whether it makes sense to update.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Spring Branch residents?
localFaqs.answer: Spring Branch is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing among Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4.

localFaqs.question: My father passed and his Spring Branch home is in his name only. My mother is still alive — what do we do?
localFaqs.answer: Texas is a community property state, so even if the deed names only your father, your mother likely has a community interest in the home that survives. Depending on whether your father had a will, the typical path is either probate of the will (or Muniment of Title if the home is paid off) or a Determination of Heirship if there is no will. We can confirm exactly which procedure applies after reviewing the deed and your father's documents.

localFaqs.question: My family is in a second marriage with kids from prior marriages. How do we plan fairly?
localFaqs.answer: Blended-family planning is common in Spring Branch, and it's one area where wills alone often fall short. A revocable living trust with carefully drafted terms — sometimes called a marital trust or QTIP structure — can let the surviving spouse use the home and assets during their lifetime while ensuring that what's left passes to the children of the first marriage. We walk through the options and trade-offs at the planning consultation.

probate-attorney-katy-tx.heroIntro: Katy sits at the intersection of three counties — Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller. Where probate is filed depends on the deceased's actual address, not the ZIP code. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles Katy probate matters in all three jurisdictions with over 25 years of experience.
probate-attorney-katy-tx.localContent:
Katy is one of the more legally complicated communities in the Houston metro because it sits at the intersection of three counties. Most of Katy and the master-planned communities around it — Cinco Ranch, Cross Creek Ranch, Firethorne, Cane Island, Falcon Landing — sit in either Harris County or Fort Bend County, with smaller pockets in Waller County to the northwest. A Katy ZIP code (77449, 77450, 77493, 77494) tells you nothing definitive about which county a particular home sits in. Where probate is filed depends on the deceased's legal residence at the time of death, confirmed by checking the actual property records.

That makes the first step in any Katy probate matter the same: confirm the correct county. We file in all three:

  • Harris County — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 at 201 Caroline Street, Houston
  • Fort Bend County — Court at Law No. 2 at 301 Jackson Street, Richmond
  • Waller County — Constitutional county court at 836 Austin Street, Hempstead

Filing in the wrong county wastes time and filing fees, and can complicate notice to creditors. Getting the jurisdiction right at the start matters. If you're administering a Katy resident's estate, contesting a will, or planning ahead, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review and we'll confirm the correct county before any filing.

localFaqs.question: I live in Katy — which county handles my probate?
localFaqs.answer: Katy spans three counties: Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller. Your ZIP code doesn't determine the county; the property records do. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation by checking the deceased's address against county property data. Most of Cinco Ranch is in Fort Bend; older Katy north of I-10 is largely in Harris; communities in northwest Katy can be in Waller.

localFaqs.question: What if probate was filed in the wrong county?
localFaqs.answer: It can be transferred, but the time and filing fees are wasted, creditor notices have to be re-issued, and the heirs lose weeks or months on the timeline. That's why confirming the correct county before filing matters. If you've already filed in the wrong county, we can help with the transfer and minimize the disruption.

localFaqs.question: How does the timeline differ between Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller for Katy residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County typically takes 4 to 8 months for an uncontested independent administration. Fort Bend tends to run 3 to 6 months. Waller, being a smaller rural county, often moves faster — 3 to 5 months. The county where the deceased lived determines which timeline applies.

localFaqs.question: I work in oil and gas and have RSUs and stock options. Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the equity plan. Vested stock that's been transferred to a brokerage account passes by beneficiary designation if one is named, otherwise through probate. Unvested RSUs and unexercised options are usually governed by the plan document — many plans accelerate vesting at death and pay to the named beneficiary, but plans differ. We review equity grant agreements as part of estate planning for Katy energy-sector clients.

probate-attorney-cinco-ranch-tx.heroIntro: Cinco Ranch is one of the largest master-planned communities in Texas — thousands of homes spread across Fort Bend County's Katy area, full of school-age families and growing assets to plan around. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has guided Cinco Ranch families through Fort Bend County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-cinco-ranch-tx.localContent:
Cinco Ranch is a sprawling master-planned community in the Katy area, sitting almost entirely in Fort Bend County (despite a Katy mailing address that often confuses people about which county actually has jurisdiction). Built across roughly 7,600 acres with dozens of villages, lakes, golf courses, and recreation centers, Cinco Ranch is anchored by Katy ISD schools and has long been one of the most popular destinations for families relocating to the Houston metro. The typical resident profile here is a working professional or dual-professional household with school-age children, a primary residence with growing equity, retirement and brokerage accounts in their accumulation phase, and frequently term life insurance tied to mortgage and education obligations.

That profile shapes the kind of estate planning work we do for Cinco Ranch families. The single most important documents for parents of minor children are a will that names guardians and a properly funded contingent trust for the children's benefit — and yet these are exactly the documents that most young Cinco Ranch families haven't gotten around to drafting. Without a guardianship designation in a will, the choice of guardian for minor children falls to the court rather than the parents. Without a children's trust, life insurance proceeds and retirement accounts can pay outright to a minor at age 18, which is rarely what parents intend.

Cinco Ranch sits in Fort Bend County, so probate matters are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond — about a 25-minute drive south. Fort Bend's docket tends to move efficiently and uncontested probate often closes in 3 to 6 months. Call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

  • Children's Trusts — to manage life insurance, retirement, and other assets for minors

localFaqs.question: Cinco Ranch has a Katy address — which county handles probate?
localFaqs.answer: Almost all of Cinco Ranch is in Fort Bend County, despite the Katy mailing address. Probate is heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation by checking the property records — Katy spans Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller counties, and the ZIP code alone doesn't determine the court.

localFaqs.question: We have young children. What's the most important estate planning document for us?
localFaqs.answer: For parents of minor children, a will that names guardians is the single most important document — without it, the court chooses who raises your children if both parents die. The second most important is a children's trust funded by life insurance and retirement beneficiary designations, so that money is managed by a trustee until the children reach an age you choose, rather than paying outright at 18. We can put both in place efficiently.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 3 months. Fort Bend's docket generally moves more quickly than Harris County's.

localFaqs.question: Our home is in Cinco Ranch and our retirement accounts have my spouse listed as primary beneficiary. Is anything else needed?
localFaqs.answer: Possibly. Beneficiary designations cover the accounts themselves, but you still need a contingent beneficiary (in case your spouse predeceases you), a will that handles everything else (the home, vehicles, personal property, bank accounts without payable-on-death designations), and powers of attorney for incapacity. Many young Cinco Ranch families have the easy half done and the rest still on the to-do list.

Cross Creek Ranch sits in Fulshear, in the rapidly growing western edge of Fort Bend County. Built by Trendmaker and Newland on roughly 3,200 acres, the community is anchored by an extensive system of lakes, creeks, and waterfront parks — about a third of the property is dedicated to open space and water — and by some of the most highly rated schools in the Houston metro. The home product runs from upper-mid-range new construction to luxury estate sections, and the typical resident profile skews toward two-income professional families, business owners, and a meaningful share of households with significant assets beyond the primary residence.

That asset profile makes the estate planning conversation more layered than just a will. We see Cross Creek Ranch families with brokerage accounts, restricted stock from publicly traded employers, S-corp or LLC interests, second homes (often in Colorado, Florida, or another state), and retirement balances large enough that beneficiary mechanics matter. Each of those instruments transfers differently, and stitching them together so the overall plan actually does what the family intends is most of the work.

Cross Creek Ranch is in Fort Bend County, so probate matters are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. localFaqs.question: We have a vacation home in Colorado. Will that go through Texas probate?
localFaqs.answer: No — out-of-state real estate is typically governed by the law of the state where the property sits, and titled real estate in another state usually requires a separate "ancillary probate" there. That means your family runs two probates instead of one. A revocable living trust holding the Colorado property usually avoids the second probate. We handle multi-state planning for Cross Creek Ranch families regularly.

localFaqs.question: I own a small business as an LLC. How does that get handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the LLC's operating agreement. Some agreements transfer member interest by beneficiary designation, some require buyout by the surviving members, and some pass the interest through probate by default. The first step is to review the operating agreement. From there, the planning question is how to coordinate the LLC interest with the rest of the estate — often with a buy-sell funded by life insurance, or by holding the LLC in a revocable trust.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months. Fort Bend's docket generally moves more quickly than Harris County's.

probate-attorney-fulshear-tx.heroIntro: Fulshear has gone from a quiet rural town of a few hundred residents to one of the fastest-growing cities in Texas — and many longtime families still hold significant land alongside the wave of new master-planned communities. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Fort Bend County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-fulshear-tx.localContent:
Few cities in the Houston metro have changed faster than Fulshear. As recently as the early 2000s, Fulshear was a small unincorporated community of cattle ranches and farmland west of Katy. Today the city's population has grown more than tenfold, anchored by master-planned communities like Cross Creek Ranch, Tamarron, Jordan Ranch, Polo Ranch, and Fulbrook on Fulshear Creek — and by ongoing residential development that shows no sign of slowing. That rapid growth has created a unique split among Fulshear families: longtime landowners whose families have held acreage in the area for generations, and the much larger group of newer residents in master-planned communities who arrived in the last decade.

Each group brings different probate and estate planning concerns. For longtime families, the issue is often inherited land where probate was handled informally — or never opened — through prior generations, leaving title clouded. We see Fulshear acreage where the deed is still in a grandparent or great-grandparent's name and the current generation needs to clear title before a sale, a development offer, or a refinance. For newer residents in master-planned communities, the planning conversation usually centers on younger families with growing assets, school-age children, and the standard set of questions about wills, guardianship, and life insurance.

Fulshear sits in Fort Bend County, so probate matters are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond — about a 30-minute drive. Uncontested probate typically closes in 3 to 6 months in Fort Bend. localFaqs.question: Our family has owned Fulshear acreage for generations and the deed is still in my grandfather's name. Can we clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated are resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is required, working back through each generation. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for Fulshear families regularly. Once title is cleared, the land can be sold, developed, or passed on properly.

localFaqs.question: Where is probate filed for Fulshear residents?
localFaqs.answer: Fulshear is in Fort Bend County, so probate is heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Filings are made through the Fort Bend County Clerk.

localFaqs.question: We're a young family in a Fulshear master-planned community with two small kids. What planning do we actually need?
localFaqs.answer: The essentials for parents of minor children are: a will that names guardians for the children, a children's trust to hold life insurance and retirement proceeds until the kids reach an age you choose (rather than paying outright at 18), durable and medical powers of attorney for each spouse, and updated beneficiary designations on retirement and life insurance. Most of this can be put in place efficiently and at fixed cost.

localFaqs.question: We sold a piece of inherited Fulshear land for development. Are there capital gains issues?
localFaqs.answer: Generally inherited property gets a step-up in tax basis to fair market value at the date of death, meaning capital gains tax is calculated on the appreciation since you inherited — not since the original purchase. That's a significant benefit for long-held Fulshear land where the original cost was minimal. The exact tax treatment depends on whether the land was titled to you outright, to a trust, or transferred during the prior owner's lifetime. Coordinate with a tax professional alongside your estate plan.

probate-attorney-firethorne-tx.heroIntro: Firethorne is a Katy-area master-planned community sitting in Fort Bend County — newer construction, family-focused, and full of households still building toward their long-term plans. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Fort Bend County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-firethorne-tx.localContent:
Firethorne is one of the master-planned communities that has grown along the FM 1463 corridor in the Katy area, on roughly 1,500 acres in northern Fort Bend County. The community was developed across two main sections — Firethorne and Firethorne West — and is anchored by Lamar Consolidated and Katy ISD schools depending on which section a home falls in. Firethorne tends to attract working professional families, energy-sector employees commuting to the Energy Corridor, and households relocating from inside the loop or out of state. The home product is mostly upper-mid-range new construction from the past decade-plus.

One thing worth knowing for Firethorne families: although the community has a Katy mailing address, almost all of it is in Fort Bend County — not Harris. That distinction matters because Katy proper actually spans three counties (Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller), and the county where probate is filed is determined by the deceased's actual property address rather than the ZIP code. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation, but the answer for Firethorne residents is almost always Fort Bend.

That makes Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 the relevant court. It sits at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond — about a 30-minute drive south of Firethorne. Fort Bend's docket moves efficiently and uncontested probate typically closes in 3 to 6 months. Whether you're administering an estate, dealing with a contested will, or putting a plan in place for a young family, call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: Firethorne has a Katy address but is it in Harris County or Fort Bend?
localFaqs.answer: Almost all of Firethorne is in Fort Bend County, despite the Katy mailing address. Probate is heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation by checking the actual property records, since Katy proper spans three counties.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Fort Bend?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months. Fort Bend generally moves more quickly than Harris County's four probate courts.

localFaqs.question: I commute to the Energy Corridor and have RSUs through my employer. Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the equity plan. Vested RSUs that have been settled into a brokerage account pass by beneficiary designation if one is named, otherwise through probate. Unvested RSUs are governed by the plan document — many plans accelerate vesting at death and pay to a named beneficiary, but plans differ. We review equity grant agreements as part of estate planning for Firethorne energy-sector clients.

localFaqs.question: Do we need a trust if our only major asset is the Firethorne home?
localFaqs.answer: Often no. For families whose primary assets are the home, retirement accounts, and life insurance, a Texas-specific will plus a Transfer on Death Deed for the home plus updated beneficiary designations is usually sufficient — and avoids the cost and complexity of trust funding. Trusts make the most sense when there's out-of-state property, a blended family, or a need to manage assets for minor or young-adult beneficiaries.

probate-attorney-westchase-houston-tx.heroIntro: Westchase sits along the Westheimer/Beltway 8 corridor — a dense business district by day and a substantial residential community after hours, with everything from corporate offices to garden-style apartments to single-family neighborhoods. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Harris County probate for Westchase residents for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-westchase-houston-tx.localContent:
Westchase is one of Houston's largest employment districts, anchored by the Westchase District improvement zone roughly framed by Beltway 8, Westheimer, Richmond, and Westpark. The mid- and high-rise office towers along Beltway 8 host major energy, engineering, healthcare, and consulting employers, and the surrounding residential mix runs from established single-family neighborhoods to townhomes to one of the largest concentrations of mid-rise apartments in the city. That combination — a major employment center with a substantial built-in residential population — produces a steady mix of probate work for our office.

One pattern we see often for Westchase residents is what we'd call the "mid-career professional gap" — clients in their 30s, 40s, and 50s who have meaningful retirement balances, employer stock, and growing home equity, but who haven't gotten around to drafting a will or updating beneficiary designations from when they first started working. The cost of that gap usually shows up only after a death or disability, and at that point options are much more limited. Putting the basic estate planning documents in place is one of the most cost-effective steps any working professional can take.

Westchase sits in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — with cases randomly assigned at filing. Whether you're administering an estate, contesting a will, or putting a plan in place, call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Westchase residents?
localFaqs.answer: Westchase is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing among Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4.

localFaqs.question: I'm a single professional in a Westchase townhome with no kids. Do I really need a will?
localFaqs.answer: Yes — arguably more than someone with a spouse and children. Without a will, Texas intestate succession determines who inherits, which usually means parents and siblings, and not necessarily in proportions you'd choose. A will lets you direct where your home, accounts, and personal property go, name an executor, and avoid the heirship proceeding that intestate succession otherwise requires. It's one of the simplest documents to put in place.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Roughly 4 to 8 months from filing to closing for an uncontested independent administration, depending on the docket of the assigned probate court and complexity of the estate. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 4 months.

localFaqs.question: My employer's 401(k) lists my parents as beneficiaries from when I started working 20 years ago. Does that matter now that I'm married?
localFaqs.answer: It can matter enormously. ERISA-governed 401(k)s require spousal consent for non-spouse beneficiaries, so a stale designation may already be overridden by federal law — but that's not always the case for IRAs, life insurance, or non-ERISA accounts. Reviewing every beneficiary designation when you marry, divorce, or have children is one of the most valuable five-minute exercises in estate planning, and we walk through it with Westchase clients regularly.

probate-attorney-briargrove-houston-tx.heroIntro: Briargrove is a quiet, deed-restricted neighborhood inside the loop — small, established, and increasingly defined by tear-down-and-rebuild new construction next to mid-century originals. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Harris County probate for Briargrove families for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-briargrove-houston-tx.localContent:
Briargrove is a tightly drawn, deed-restricted neighborhood just west of the Galleria, framed by Westheimer, the West Loop, San Felipe, and Fountain View. The community was platted in the 1950s and 60s with single-story ranch homes on generous lots, and for decades it remained one of Houston's quieter, more affordable inner-loop options. That has changed substantially in the last twenty years. Today Briargrove is one of the most active tear-down-and-rebuild neighborhoods inside the loop, with original mid-century homes being replaced by larger custom builds — and the resulting mix of long-tenured original owners alongside newer high-end residents shapes the kind of probate and estate planning work we do here.

For long-tenured Briargrove owners, the recurring planning issue is the dramatic appreciation in their homes. A house bought for $30,000 or $40,000 fifty years ago may now be worth well over a million as a tear-down lot. The federal step-up in basis at death is one of the most valuable tax features available to inherited Briargrove homes, and we coordinate planning with that benefit in mind. For newer residents — often professionals or business owners who paid current market prices — the focus is more typical of Houston's affluent inner-loop areas: trusts, asset coordination, charitable planning, and clear instructions for executors.

Briargrove sits in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — with cases randomly assigned at filing. Whether you're administering an estate or planning the transfer of a long-held home, call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for paid-off Briargrove homes with no unpaid debts

  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court

localFaqs.question: My parents bought their Briargrove home in the 1960s. Should they put it in our names now to avoid probate?
localFaqs.answer: Almost never — and this is one of the most common avoidable mistakes we see. Transferring a long-held home to children during the parents' lifetime gives up the step-up in basis at death, which can mean hundreds of thousands of dollars in extra capital gains tax when the home is eventually sold. A Texas Transfer on Death Deed or a revocable living trust accomplishes the same probate-avoidance goal while preserving the step-up. We talk this through with Briargrove families regularly.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Briargrove residents?
localFaqs.answer: Briargrove is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing among Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4.

localFaqs.question: Briargrove has deed restrictions. Do those affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: Deed restrictions don't change probate procedure, but they do bind the heirs once title transfers — including restrictions on use, setbacks, and architectural review. If a beneficiary plans to tear down and rebuild, the architectural committee process and any current restrictions need to be factored in. We're happy to flag any restrictions that might affect the heirs' plans during the administration.

localFaqs.question: Our home has appreciated to over a million dollars. Do we have a federal estate tax problem?
localFaqs.answer: Probably not, at current exemption levels. The federal estate tax exemption is currently in the multi-million-dollar range per person and is portable between spouses, meaning most Briargrove families well below that threshold owe no federal estate tax even on substantial estates. That said, the exemption is scheduled to revisit Congress periodically, and very-high-net-worth households may want to coordinate with a tax professional. For most Briargrove residents the planning is about administration efficiency and capital gains, not estate tax.

probate-attorney-tanglewood-houston-tx.heroIntro: Tanglewood is one of Houston's quietest and most established affluent neighborhoods — old money, longtime families, and the neighborhood the Bush family called home for decades. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Harris County probate and estate planning for Tanglewood families for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-tanglewood-houston-tx.localContent:
Tanglewood sits just west of the West Loop and immediately south of the Memorial Villages, framed by San Felipe, Chimney Rock, Woodway, and 610. The neighborhood was developed in the 1940s and 50s on what had been the Tanglewood ranch, with deed restrictions designed to maintain large lots and a quiet, residential character. That character has held up unusually well — Tanglewood has remained one of Houston's most established and least transitional affluent neighborhoods, with many families having lived in their homes for decades. Former President George H.W. Bush and Barbara Bush were Tanglewood residents for decades, which gives some sense of the neighborhood's character.

The estate planning work we do for Tanglewood families is correspondingly more layered than what a routine probate page describes. Many residents have substantial estates with brokerage and retirement accounts, business and partnership interests, vacation homes in Texas Hill Country or out of state, art and collectibles, and increasingly, a meaningful charitable component to their plans. The planning conversation often touches on revocable trusts, marital deduction planning, generation-skipping considerations for grandchildren, family limited partnerships, charitable remainder trusts or donor-advised funds, and the coordination of all of those with each other and with a Texas-specific will. None of that reduces the importance of the basics — a current will, durable powers of attorney, medical directives, properly-titled real estate, updated beneficiary designations — which are still the foundation of any Tanglewood family's plan.

Tanglewood sits in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — with cases randomly assigned at filing. Call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will

  • Ancillary Probate — for clients with vacation homes or property in other states

  • Durable Power of Attorney

localFaqs.answer: Tanglewood is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned at filing among Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4.

localFaqs.question: Should our estate plan use a revocable living trust instead of just a will?
localFaqs.answer: For Tanglewood families with property in multiple states, blended families, or privacy concerns about probate filings, a revocable living trust is often worth considering. Trusts avoid probate entirely (which means filings stay private), handle out-of-state property without ancillary probate, and provide built-in management if the grantor becomes incapacitated. They cost more to draft and require ongoing attention to keep funded, so the choice is fact-specific. We walk through the trade-offs at the planning consultation.

localFaqs.question: My elderly mother amended her longstanding Tanglewood will recently to favor one child. Can we contest it?
localFaqs.answer: Possibly. Late-in-life amendments to longstanding wills, particularly when one child has become primary caregiver, are one of the most common fact patterns in undue-influence litigation. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate, and earlier action almost always strengthens the case. We can review the documents, timeline, and circumstances and give you an honest assessment.

localFaqs.question: We have a Hill Country ranch in Llano County. Does that get handled in our Tanglewood probate?
localFaqs.answer: If both properties are in Texas, generally yes — a single Texas probate handles real estate anywhere in the state. The complication arises if any real estate is in another state, in which case that state typically requires a separate "ancillary probate" on top of the Texas administration. A revocable living trust holding any out-of-state property usually avoids that.

probate-attorney-piney-point-tx.localContent:
Piney Point Village is one of the six small incorporated cities — collectively the "Memorial Villages" — that sit inside Houston between Memorial Drive and I-10. The village covers roughly two square miles, with a population of about 3,300 residents, large lots typically over an acre, and homes that range from longtime mid-century originals to substantial new estate builds. Median household income consistently ranks among the highest in the state. Piney Point has its own city government, police department, and zoning rules — and the city's character is defined by mature pine and oak canopy, deep front yards, and a deliberate effort to preserve a low-density residential feel inside one of the largest cities in the country.

For probate purposes, the most important thing for Piney Point residents to understand is that even though the village is its own incorporated city, it is still part of Harris County. There is no Piney Point probate court. All probate matters for Piney Point residents are heard by the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. We mention this because residents occasionally assume their city government handles matters that actually fall to the county — vehicle registration, court filings, property records — and probate falls firmly on the county side.

The estate planning needs of Piney Point families tend to be more sophisticated than a typical Harris County probate page would address. Multi-generational wealth, family entities, charitable planning, and coordinated trust structures are common, and the planning conversation often involves both estate counsel and a tax advisor. Whether you're administering a parent's estate, dealing with a contested will or trust, or building a plan for the next generation, call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: Piney Point Village has its own city government — does it have its own probate court?
localFaqs.answer: No. Piney Point Village is an incorporated city, but it is still part of Harris County for court purposes. All probate matters for Piney Point residents are heard by the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. The same is true for the other Memorial Villages — Hunters Creek, Bunker Hill, Hedwig, Hilshire, and Spring Valley. The village governments handle local services and zoning; the county handles courts.

localFaqs.question: Our family has trusts going back two generations. How do we administer everything together?
localFaqs.answer: Multi-trust administrations call for careful sequencing. The first step is usually to inventory each trust, locate the current trust documents and any amendments, identify the trustees and successor trustees, and confirm each trust's holdings and beneficiaries. From there, we coordinate accountings, distributions, and any required notices to beneficiaries. We handle multi-trust matters for Memorial Villages families regularly and coordinate with tax counsel where the trust returns are complex.

localFaqs.question: Does Texas have its own estate tax for high-value estates?
localFaqs.answer: No. Texas has no state-level estate tax, gift tax, or inheritance tax. The only estate tax that applies is the federal estate tax, which has a generous exemption per person and is portable between spouses. For Piney Point families with substantial estates, federal estate tax planning is sometimes relevant — generation-skipping considerations, charitable structures, family entities — but those decisions are best made in coordination with both estate counsel and a tax advisor.

localFaqs.question: We're concerned about privacy. Is there a way to administer an estate without filings becoming public?
localFaqs.answer: To a meaningful extent, yes. Probate filings are public record, but assets that pass through a properly funded revocable living trust avoid probate entirely — meaning the trust assets, distributions, and beneficiaries do not appear on the public docket. For Piney Point families with privacy concerns, a revocable trust is often the planning tool of choice. The trust must actually hold the assets to provide that privacy, which means careful funding during the grantor's lifetime.

The city covers roughly two square miles, has a population of about 4,500 residents, and is defined by the same broad characteristics as its neighboring villages — large lots, mature oak and pine canopy, predominantly single-family character, and consistently high household incomes. Hunters Creek was incorporated in 1954 in part to control land use within the community, and that careful zoning has shaped the city's residential feel ever since. The home product runs from longtime mid-century originals on generous lots to substantial new construction on the same footprints.

One thing worth knowing for Hunters Creek residents is that the village's status as an incorporated city does not change the court that handles probate. Hunters Creek is still part of Harris County, and probate matters for Hunters Creek residents are heard by the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. The planning conversation for Hunters Creek families tends to look more like the conversation in any of the other Memorial Villages — substantial estates, multi-generational wealth in some cases, family entities, real estate beyond the primary residence, charitable planning, and the coordination of all of it with each other. localFaqs.question: Hunters Creek is an incorporated city. Does it have its own probate court?
localFaqs.answer: No. Hunters Creek Village is an incorporated city, but it is still part of Harris County for court purposes. Probate matters for Hunters Creek residents are heard by the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. The village government handles local services like police and zoning; the county handles courts and probate.

localFaqs.question: Should our family use a revocable living trust instead of just a will?
localFaqs.answer: For Hunters Creek families with substantial estates, out-of-state property, or privacy concerns, a revocable living trust is often worth considering. Trusts avoid probate entirely (keeping the inventory and distributions off the public docket), handle out-of-state real estate without ancillary probate, and provide built-in management if the grantor becomes incapacitated. They require careful funding to be effective. We walk through the trade-offs at the planning consultation.

localFaqs.question: We have a successor trustee named, but no one has briefed her on what the role involves. Is that a problem?
localFaqs.answer: It's a common and avoidable problem. Successor trustees take on significant fiduciary duties — investment, accounting, distribution, communication with beneficiaries, tax filing — and stepping into the role cold can be overwhelming. We strongly recommend that successor trustees be briefed on the trust terms and their duties during the grantor's lifetime, and that they have a relationship with counsel they can call when the time comes. A short planning meeting now prevents a lot of mistakes later.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Roughly 4 to 8 months from filing to closing for an uncontested independent administration, depending on the docket of the assigned probate court and complexity of the estate. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 4 months. Contested cases or complex estates take longer.

probate-attorney-bunker-hill-village-tx.heroIntro: Bunker Hill Village is one of the six Memorial Villages — a small incorporated city inside Houston with consistently among the highest median household incomes in Texas. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Harris County probate for Memorial Village families for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-bunker-hill-village-tx.localContent:
Bunker Hill Village is the geographic and, in many years, the economic core of the Memorial Villages — a small incorporated city of roughly 3,800 residents covering about two square miles, framed by Memorial Drive, I-10, and the surrounding Hunters Creek and Piney Point villages. Bunker Hill is regularly listed among the highest-median-household-income cities in Texas, and the residential character reflects that — large wooded lots, deep setbacks, mature canopy, and homes ranging from longtime mid-century originals to substantial new estate builds. The city was incorporated in 1954, the same era as several of the other villages, partly to control land use and preserve the low-density residential character that defines the area today.

For Bunker Hill residents, the probate jurisdiction question matters because the village is genuinely its own incorporated city — but it is still part of Harris County. There is no Bunker Hill probate court. All probate matters for Bunker Hill residents are heard by the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. The village government handles local services like police, public works, and zoning; the county handles courts, vehicle registration, property records, and probate.

The planning conversation for Bunker Hill families is typically among the most layered we have. Multi-generational wealth, family limited partnerships, real estate in Texas Hill Country or out of state, art and collectible inventories, charitable structures, generation-skipping considerations, and the coordination of trustees and successor trustees across multiple instruments are all common topics. Whether you're administering an estate, dealing with a contested will or trust, or building out the next generation's plan, call us at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Bunker Hill Village Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in the Harris County Probate Courts:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Ancillary Probate — for families with property in other states

Estate Planning for Bunker Hill Families

For substantial estates, the plan needs to coordinate the will with everything that passes outside the will. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for privacy, multi-state property, blended families, and incapacity management
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Bunker Hill home out of probate
  • Coordination with tax counsel on charitable giving structures, family entities, and generation-skipping planning where appropriate

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills, contested trusts, and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts — including disputes among adult children over executor or trustee conduct and complex multi-trust accountings. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Bunker Hill families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Bunker Hill Village and the Memorial Villages

Our office serves clients throughout Bunker Hill Village, Hunters Creek Village, Piney Point Village, Hedwig Village, Hilshire Village, Spring Valley Village, Memorial, and surrounding Harris County communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.
