localFaqs.question: Bunker Hill Village is its own incorporated city. Where does probate happen?
localFaqs.answer: Despite being its own incorporated city, Bunker Hill Village is part of Harris County for court purposes. Probate matters for Bunker Hill residents are heard by the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, with cases randomly assigned at filing. The village government handles local services and zoning; the county handles courts and probate.

localFaqs.question: Our parents have a family limited partnership holding real estate and securities. How does that get handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: Family limited partnerships generally don't dissolve at the death of a partner — the partner's interest in the FLP passes through their estate (by will or trust) to the named beneficiaries, who then become limited partners. The actual partnership assets stay inside the entity. The handling depends on the partnership agreement, the estate documents, and whether the FLP interest was held in a revocable trust or in the deceased's individual name. We coordinate FLP administration with tax counsel as part of the broader estate.

localFaqs.question: We're concerned about privacy. Can we administer an estate without it becoming public?
localFaqs.answer: To a significant extent, yes. Probate filings are public record, but assets passing through a properly funded revocable living trust avoid probate entirely — meaning the inventory, distributions, and beneficiary identities never appear on the public docket. For Bunker Hill families with privacy concerns, a fully funded revocable trust is often the planning tool of choice. Funding the trust during the grantor's lifetime is what makes the privacy benefit work.

localFaqs.question: Does Texas tax estates or inheritances at the state level?
localFaqs.answer: No. Texas has no estate tax, gift tax, or inheritance tax. The only estate tax that may apply is the federal estate tax, which has a generous exemption per person (currently in the multi-million-dollar range) and is portable between spouses. Most Bunker Hill families fall below the threshold even on substantial estates, but very-high-net-worth households should coordinate with tax counsel on federal exposure and any planning that may reduce it.

probate-attorney-sugar-land-tx.heroIntro: Sugar Land sits at the heart of Fort Bend County — one of the most diverse and high-income counties in Texas. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has guided Sugar Land families through Fort Bend probate and estate planning for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-sugar-land-tx.localContent:
Sugar Land has grown from a sugar refinery town into one of the most prosperous and diverse cities in Texas. Today the city is built around master-planned communities — First Colony, Sweetwater, Greatwood, Riverstone, Telfair, Avalon, Sienna (just south, in Missouri City) — many of them home to professionals, business owners, and families with significant assets to plan around. Fort Bend County has consistently ranked among the highest-income counties in the state, and that shapes the kind of estate planning we do for Sugar Land residents: more frequently we're dealing with multi-state assets, business interests, charitable giving, and blended families.

Probate matters for Fort Bend County residents are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. Filings are made through the Fort Bend County Clerk. Fort Bend's docket tends to move faster than Harris County's, and uncontested probate often closes in 3 to 6 months for our Sugar Land clients.

If you need to probate a will in Fort Bend County, open an heirship proceeding for a relative who died without a will, contest a will or trust, or build out an estate plan that protects your home, business, and family, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: Which court hears probate matters for Sugar Land residents?
localFaqs.answer: Probate cases for Fort Bend County residents — including Sugar Land — are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Filings are made through the Fort Bend County Clerk.

localFaqs.question: Is probate faster in Fort Bend County than in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes. Fort Bend's docket carries a smaller volume than Harris County's four probate courts combined, and uncontested independent administrations often close in 3 to 6 months in Fort Bend, compared with 4 to 8 months in Harris. Complex or contested cases take longer in either county.

localFaqs.question: Sugar Land has master-planned communities with HOAs — does that affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: HOAs themselves don't change probate procedure, but assessments continue to accrue during administration, and most associations require notice of the homeowner's death and proof of executor authority before they'll communicate with heirs. We help Sugar Land executors handle that notice and protect against late fees or liens.

localFaqs.question: I'm a Sugar Land resident with property in another state. Do I need a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes. Real property in another state typically triggers a separate "ancillary probate" in that state if it's titled in the deceased's individual name — meaning your family runs two probates instead of one. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that. We can review your situation and tell you whether a trust makes sense.

Established neighborhoods like Quail Valley sit alongside more recent master-planned communities — Lake Olympia, Riverstone (just over the line in Sugar Land), and the enormous Sienna community to the south. Many of our Missouri City clients are first- or second-generation Americans, with strong ties to communities in Nigeria, India, the Philippines, China, and the Caribbean. The probate division can be reached at (281) 341-4443. Fort Bend's docket tends to move efficiently, and uncontested probate often closes in 3 to 6 months for our Missouri City clients.

localFaqs.question: Which court hears probate matters for Missouri City residents?
localFaqs.answer: Most of Missouri City sits in Fort Bend County, so probate cases are filed with the Fort Bend County Clerk and heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. A small portion of Missouri City extends into Harris County — for those addresses, probate is heard at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation.

localFaqs.question: My parents own property in Nigeria and a home here in Missouri City. How do we handle both at death?
localFaqs.answer: Texas probate only governs Texas-situs assets. Foreign real property is generally controlled by the law of the country where it sits, and a separate probate or inheritance proceeding is usually required there. We coordinate the Texas probate or trust administration and work alongside foreign counsel where needed. Putting U.S. assets in a revocable living trust and addressing foreign property under that country's planning rules is often the cleanest approach — we'll talk through what fits your family.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 4 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: I'm an heir but I live overseas. Can probate still proceed without me coming to Texas?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes. Heirs and beneficiaries usually do not need to appear in person; named executors do for the initial hearing, but Fort Bend allows many subsequent appearances by Zoom. We routinely represent overseas heirs and out-of-country executors and can serve as Texas registered agent, handle filings, and coordinate communications so travel is limited to what's strictly necessary.

probate-attorney-stafford-tx.heroIntro: Stafford is the small Fort Bend city famous for charging no city property tax — a quirk that, along with its working-class to middle-class neighborhoods and steady manufacturing base, gives Stafford a real character of its own. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has handled Fort Bend probate matters for Stafford families for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-stafford-tx.localContent:
Stafford is unlike any other city in the Houston metro. Sitting along U.S. 59 between Sugar Land and Missouri City, Stafford famously eliminated its city property tax in 1995 — a policy made possible by the city's strong sales-tax base from manufacturing, light industry, and retail along the corridor. That makes Stafford one of the few places in Texas where homeowners pay only county, school district, and special-purpose property taxes. The result is a community of working-class and middle-class families, many of them long-tenured, with a steady mix of small homes, townhomes, and older single-family neighborhoods. Many Stafford residents are first- or second-generation Americans with roots in Asia, Africa, Latin America, and the Caribbean.

Stafford sits in Fort Bend County, so probate matters for Stafford residents are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond — about a 20-minute drive west. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Fort Bend's docket runs efficiently, and uncontested probate often closes in 3 to 6 months.

If you're administering an estate, dealing with a contested will, or putting an estate plan in place that protects a modest home and a working family's savings, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: Where do I file probate for a Stafford resident?
localFaqs.answer: Stafford is in Fort Bend County, so probate cases are filed with the Fort Bend County Clerk and heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443.

localFaqs.question: Stafford has no city property tax — does that affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: Not directly. Stafford's no-city-property-tax policy is a city revenue choice; it doesn't change probate procedure or homestead rules. What it can mean for our clients is that Stafford homes are slightly more affordable to hold during a long administration, since the carrying cost is a bit lower than equivalent property in neighboring cities. School district, county, and special district taxes still apply and still need to be paid by the executor during probate.

localFaqs.question: My mother left a paid-off house and about $40,000 in a checking account — do we need full probate?
localFaqs.answer: Possibly not. If the non-real-property assets are under $75,000 and there's no will, a Small Estate Affidavit may be available. If she had a will and the only debt is a mortgage on the home, a Muniment of Title is typically faster and cheaper than full administration. Either route saves time and legal fees compared with a full independent administration. We'll tell you honestly which fits.

localFaqs.question: Do I need a lawyer to file probate in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: For most probate filings in Texas, yes — the executor or applicant is generally considered to be representing the estate's interests, not just their own, so the courts require an attorney for full administration and for Determinations of Heirship. Small Estate Affidavits and a few other simplified procedures can sometimes be filed without counsel. We'll tell you up front whether your situation is one where you can handle it yourself.

probate-attorney-richmond-tx.heroIntro: Richmond is the county seat of Fort Bend County — and home to the Justice Center on Jackson Street, where every Fort Bend probate matter is heard. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has filed probate matters in Richmond for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-richmond-tx.localContent:
Richmond is the county seat of Fort Bend County and one of the oldest communities in the Houston region — chartered in 1837, just a year after Texas independence. The historic district along Morton Street still has the courthouse-square feel that has all but disappeared in most of the metro, while subdivisions like Pecan Grove, Aliana, Long Meadow Farms, Harvest Green, and Veranda have brought rapid residential growth around the city's edges. The Brazos River runs right through town, and Richmond's mix of historic buildings, working downtown, and master-planned communities makes it both the legal heart of the county and one of its most active growth areas.

For Richmond residents, the probate court is right downtown. Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 sits at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street, with filings made through the Fort Bend County Clerk in the same complex. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Fort Bend has a single dedicated probate-jurisdiction court, the docket is well-managed, and uncontested administrations typically close in 3 to 6 months.

localFaqs.question: Where is the Fort Bend County probate court?

localFaqs.question: We have a piece of family property in Richmond that's been passed down without probate — can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship. Where multiple generations have died without probate — common for older Richmond family land — sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed. It's solvable and we handle these cases regularly.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 4 months. Because Fort Bend has a single dedicated probate-jurisdiction court, the docket is generally predictable.

localFaqs.question: Does living in the county seat give me any advantage in probate?
localFaqs.answer: Practically, yes — though limited. The court is closer for in-person filings and hearings, and county clerk records are easier to access. Most of the procedural advantages, however, come from the lawyer's familiarity with the specific judge and clerk's office, not the client's residence. We file in Fort Bend regularly and know the local procedures well.

localFaqs.question: My father owned land in Mexico and a home here in Rosenberg. What happens to each?
localFaqs.answer: The Texas home goes through a Texas probate (or transfers outside probate if there's a TOD deed, joint survivorship, or a trust). The Mexican property is governed by Mexican law and typically requires a separate succession proceeding there — Mexico does not generally recognize a U.S. will as automatically transferring Mexican real property. We handle the Texas side and coordinate with Mexican counsel where useful. Planning ahead with both U.S. and Mexican advisors can prevent a long and costly probate on each side.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?

localFaqs.question: Do you handle consultations in Spanish?

The legal documents themselves — wills, trusts, court filings — must be in English under Texas law, but we make sure clients fully understand every term before signing.

probate-attorney-pecan-grove-tx.heroIntro: Pecan Grove is one of Fort Bend County's most established master-planned communities — anchored by the Pecan Grove Country Club and a generation of long-tenured homeowners. probate-attorney-pecan-grove-tx.localContent:
Pecan Grove sits in unincorporated Fort Bend County, just east of Richmond and northwest of Sugar Land. The community grew up around the Pecan Grove Plantation Country Club and golf course in the late 1970s and early 1980s, making it one of the older master-planned communities in the county. Many of the original residents are still here, and Pecan Grove has shifted into a community of long-tenured homeowners — families that bought in 30 or 40 years ago, raised children, and are now thinking about how to pass the home, the retirement accounts, and the family memories on to the next generation. That tenure shapes the kind of estate planning we do here. Many Pecan Grove clients are now in their 70s and 80s, and the questions are practical: Will my house need to go through probate? Who handles things if I can't? How do I keep my kids from fighting?

localFaqs.question: Pecan Grove is unincorporated — does that change anything for probate?
localFaqs.answer: For probate purposes, no. Probate jurisdiction is determined by the county of legal residence, not by city limits. Pecan Grove sits in Fort Bend County, so probate cases go to Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 in Richmond. What being unincorporated does mean is that the county handles most permitting and code enforcement, and there's no city government layer to deal with during administration.

localFaqs.question: I've owned my Pecan Grove home for 35 years. Is a Transfer on Death Deed enough, or do I need a trust?
localFaqs.answer: For many long-tenured Pecan Grove homeowners with a paid-off house and straightforward heirs, a Transfer on Death Deed plus a basic will is plenty. A revocable living trust adds value when you own real estate in another state, when you have a blended family, or when you want to manage how heirs receive assets over time. We'll walk through your specific situation honestly — most Pecan Grove clients don't need a trust, and we'll tell you if you don't.

localFaqs.question: My father is in his 80s and the family is worried about his neighbor's adult child influencing him on his will. What can we do?
localFaqs.answer: This is one of the most common will contest scenarios we see. While he's still alive, the most effective step is often updating his estate plan with proper safeguards — independent witnesses, capacity documentation, sometimes a trust with an institutional co-trustee. After death, a will procured by undue influence can be contested, but contests are harder, more expensive, and emotionally costly. Acting now, while he's competent, is almost always better. We're glad to talk it through with the family.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months. A Muniment of Title proceeding — often the right fit for a Pecan Grove home with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage — can close in 2 to 4 months.

localFaqs.question: My retirement accounts are larger than the rest of my estate combined. Does my will even matter?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, but maybe less than you think for those specific accounts. Retirement accounts and life insurance pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning the named beneficiary controls regardless of what the will says. Your will still governs the home, brokerage accounts without TOD designations, personal property, and anything else that doesn't have a beneficiary form. The most common avoidable mistake we see is an outdated beneficiary form (ex-spouse, predeceased relative). Reviewing those is one of the highest-value pieces of estate planning we do for Greatwood clients.

localFaqs.question: We bought a vacation home in Colorado. Will it go through Texas probate?
localFaqs.answer: No — real property is governed by the law of the state where it sits. Texas-resident decedents who own Colorado real estate generally need a Texas probate for their Texas property and a separate "ancillary probate" in Colorado for the vacation home. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids the second probate entirely. We routinely structure these for Greatwood clients.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 4 months.

localFaqs.question: I'm an OCI cardholder with property in India and a home in Riverstone. How do I plan for both?
localFaqs.answer: U.S. and Indian succession laws operate independently and need parallel planning. Your Texas home and U.S. accounts can be handled with a Texas will or trust. Indian real property is generally controlled by Indian succession law (which differs by religion, with separate Hindu, Muslim, Christian, and Parsi rules), and a separate Indian will is usually advisable so the property doesn't get caught up in lengthy Indian probate. We coordinate the U.S. side and work with Indian counsel where useful — and we make sure your two wills don't accidentally revoke each other.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. Cases involving foreign assets or out-of-country heirs can take longer due to authentication and coordination, but the Texas portion usually still moves on the standard timeline.

localFaqs.question: I want to send a substantial gift to relatives in another country. Does that affect my U.S. estate plan?
localFaqs.answer: Possibly, especially for larger gifts. The U.S. has annual exclusion and lifetime exemption rules for gifts that affect the eventual estate tax calculation, and gifts of U.S. real property or cash from U.S. accounts may have reporting obligations (Form 709 and, for some recipients, Form 3520). The recipient country may have its own gift or inheritance tax. We coordinate with your tax advisor to make sure cross-border gifts are structured cleanly.

localFaqs.question: I have young kids — what happens to them if both parents die without a will?
localFaqs.answer: The Fort Bend probate court must appoint guardians, and the court — not you — chooses among willing relatives. Family disputes over guardianship are unfortunately common, and judges have to weigh competing applications without knowing what the parents would have wanted. A simple will with named guardian designations avoids that. It's the single most important estate planning step for young Sienna parents, and it's usually fairly quick to put in place.

localFaqs.question: If my children inherit life insurance, can they get it directly?
localFaqs.answer: Not until age 18 in Texas, and then yes — outright. For most Sienna families, that's a problem. An 18-year-old receiving a $500,000 life insurance check generally isn't equipped to manage it. The fix is naming a testamentary trust as the contingent beneficiary, with a trustee you choose managing distributions until the children reach an age you select (often 25, 30, or staggered). It's a small drafting step with a large impact.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. Cases involving guardianship of minors take longer because of the additional court oversight that's required for the children's interests.

localFaqs.question: We can't find my mother's will. She lived in First Colony for 35 years. Now what?
localFaqs.answer: If a diligent search of the home, safe deposit box, and her attorney's office turns up nothing, the law presumes a will that can't be found was destroyed by the testator with intent to revoke. The estate is then administered as if she died without a will — through a Determination of Heirship and intestate distribution under Texas law. In some cases a copy of the lost will can be probated if you can prove its contents and that it was not intentionally destroyed. We handle both situations.

localFaqs.question: My will was written in 1995. Does it still work?
localFaqs.answer: Probably valid, but probably outdated. Wills from the 1990s often name guardians for now-adult children, executors who have predeceased, accounts that have been closed, and provisions that don't take advantage of modern tools like Transfer on Death Deeds, Texas independent administration improvements, or beneficiary designations on retirement accounts. We routinely refresh older wills for First Colony residents — usually for less than the cost of running an old will through unnecessary complications at probate.

localFaqs.question: I'm a Chinese citizen on a green card with property in Shanghai and a home in Telfair. What happens at death?
localFaqs.answer: Each property is generally governed by the law of the country where it sits. Your Texas home is handled through Texas probate or a Texas-based plan; the Shanghai property is governed by Chinese inheritance law and typically requires a separate notarial inheritance proceeding in China. We handle the U.S. side and coordinate with Chinese counsel where useful. A common approach is parallel wills — one for U.S. assets, one for China — drafted carefully so they don't accidentally revoke each other. We make sure those mesh.

localFaqs.question: How does U.S. estate tax apply to non-citizens with green cards?
localFaqs.answer: Green-card holders are generally treated as U.S. residents for estate tax purposes — meaning worldwide assets are included in the estate and the full federal exemption applies (currently in the multi-million range). Non-resident non-citizens face a much lower exemption (around $60,000 for U.S.-situs assets) but are taxed only on U.S.-situs property. The status matters considerably for high-net-worth Telfair families. We coordinate with tax counsel on these issues.

For Aliana's substantial Indian-American population, planning typically spans two countries and two legal systems. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament — coordinated with any Indian will
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful when you own property in India or hold significant NRE/NRO accounts
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Aliana home out of probate
  • Coordination with Indian counsel on succession certificates, NRI/OCI status issues, agricultural land restrictions, and FEMA repatriation rules

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Fort Bend County

We litigate contested wills, trust contests, and fiduciary breach claims in Fort Bend County. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

localFaqs.question: Where is probate filed for an Aliana resident?
localFaqs.answer: Aliana is in Richmond, Fort Bend County, so probate cases are filed with the Fort Bend County Clerk and heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Aliana is only about 10 minutes from the courthouse.

localFaqs.question: I'm an OCI cardholder with property and accounts in India. Should I have one will or two?
localFaqs.answer: Generally two — one Texas will for U.S. assets and a separate Indian will for Indian-situs assets — drafted carefully so neither revokes the other. Indian succession is complicated by religion-specific rules (Hindu, Muslim, Christian, and Parsi succession laws each differ), and Indian probate of a foreign will is slow and expensive. A separate Indian will, properly executed under Indian law, is usually faster for the Indian assets. We handle the Texas side and coordinate with Indian counsel.

localFaqs.question: My elderly parents in India want to gift money to us. Are there U.S. tax issues?
localFaqs.answer: For the recipient side, gifts from non-U.S. persons over $100,000 in a calendar year must be reported on IRS Form 3520 (no tax, but a reporting requirement, with significant penalties for failure). For the sender side, India has FEMA limits on outward remittance from India to the U.S. — the Liberalised Remittance Scheme allows up to $250,000 per Indian resident per year, with documentation. Larger gifts require careful structuring. We coordinate with both U.S. tax and Indian counsel on these.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Fort Bend County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Fort Bend County typically close in 3 to 6 months. Cases involving overseas heirs or Indian asset coordination can take longer for the cross-border pieces, but the Texas portion usually still moves on the standard timeline.

probate-attorney-sharpstown-houston-tx.heroIntro: Sharpstown is one of Houston's most diverse neighborhoods — anchored by Bellaire Boulevard's "Asia Town" corridor and an active Hispanic community. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has helped Sharpstown families navigate Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-sharpstown-houston-tx.localContent:
Sharpstown is one of Houston's most internationally diverse neighborhoods. Developed in the 1950s as one of the original master-planned communities in the country, Sharpstown went through a tough period in the late 20th century but has been substantially revitalized in the last two decades. The Bellaire Boulevard corridor through Sharpstown — sometimes called "Asia Town" or "Chinatown," though the Vietnamese, Chinese, Korean, Indian, and Hispanic communities all have a strong presence — is one of the most vibrant immigrant business districts in Texas. Many of our Sharpstown clients are first-generation Americans, often small-business owners with assets that span the U.S. and another country. The questions are practical: how does my family handle the restaurant if I die? What about my parents' apartment in Vietnam? Can my kids inherit even though they were born here?

Sharpstown is in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned among the four courts, and each judge runs a slightly different docket.

If you're administering an estate, contesting a will, or putting a plan in place for a family business and a home, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Sharpstown Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in the Harris County Probate Courts:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Sharpstown homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will, common where a parent dies suddenly without estate planning
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Sharpstown Families

For small-business owners and first-generation American families, a clear plan does a lot of work. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful when you own a small business, multiple rental properties, or property abroad
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Sharpstown home out of probate
  • Coordination with foreign-asset planning where families have property or accounts abroad
  • Business succession planning for restaurant, retail, or service businesses

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

We litigate contested wills, trust contests, and fiduciary breach claims in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Serving Sharpstown and Southwest Houston

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the Harris County probate court for Sharpstown residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County has four statutory probate courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — all located at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at the time of filing, so you don't choose your court. Sharpstown is about 20-25 minutes from the courthouse depending on traffic.

localFaqs.question: My parents own a small restaurant in Sharpstown. What happens to it if they die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on how the business is held. A sole proprietorship dies with the owner — the equipment and lease typically go through probate. An LLC or corporation continues, but the ownership interests pass through probate unless held in a trust or with right-of-survivorship language in the operating agreement. The day-to-day issue is usually whether someone in the family can keep operating during probate. We can put together a plan that names a successor manager, addresses the lease, and keeps the doors open through the transition.

localFaqs.question: I came to the U.S. from Vietnam and my parents still live there. If they die, can I inherit?
localFaqs.answer: Yes — U.S. citizenship has no effect on your right to inherit under Vietnamese (or any other country's) succession law. The reverse is also true: your Vietnamese-resident parents can inherit U.S. assets from you. The complications are practical: getting Vietnamese inheritance documents recognized in the U.S. (and vice versa), repatriating funds, and tax reporting. We coordinate with Vietnamese counsel where useful and handle the Texas piece.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months.

probate-attorney-bellaire-tx.heroIntro: Bellaire — "the City of Homes" — is a small, affluent independent city sitting entirely inside Houston, Harris County. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Bellaire families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-bellaire-tx.localContent:
The City of Bellaire is a small, independent municipality of about 17,000 people, sitting entirely inside the larger City of Houston in Harris County. Established in 1908 and incorporated in 1918, Bellaire has long been known as the "City of Homes" — almost entirely residential, with a strong family character, top-rated Houston ISD-zoned schools (Horn Elementary, Pin Oak Middle, Bellaire High), and a substantial Jewish community anchored by the Evelyn Rubenstein Jewish Community Center on Loop 610. Many Bellaire homes have been in the same family for two or three generations; others have been bought down, torn down, and rebuilt as larger custom homes. The result is a neighborhood with both long-tenured estate planning needs and the high-asset profile of a small affluent city.

Bellaire residents are sometimes surprised to learn that their city has its own mayor, council, police, and fire department — but probate is still handled by Harris County. The City of Bellaire is a Texas home-rule municipality, but probate jurisdiction follows the county, not the city. So matters for Bellaire residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown.

If you're administering an estate, dealing with a will contest, or putting an estate plan in place for a Bellaire home and the family it holds, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Bellaire Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in the Harris County Probate Courts:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Bellaire homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for paid-off legacy homes
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Bellaire Families

Bellaire's combination of long-tenured residents, high-value real estate, and family-business presence means estate planning here often deserves a more layered approach. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful for owners of multiple properties, family businesses, and clients with property outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Bellaire home out of probate

localFaqs.question: Bellaire has its own city government — does that mean probate is handled differently?
localFaqs.answer: No. The City of Bellaire is a separate municipality from Houston, but probate jurisdiction in Texas follows the county, not the city. Bellaire is in Harris County, so cases are filed with the Harris County Clerk and heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street downtown. The city's independence affects municipal matters (zoning, police, code enforcement), not probate.

localFaqs.question: Our home has been in the family since 1965 and was never put in a trust. What happens when my parents die?
localFaqs.answer: If the deed is in your parents' names individually, the home goes through probate (or transfers via Muniment of Title if the only debt is a mortgage). For long-tenured Bellaire homes, the property tax homestead exemption sometimes survives to a surviving spouse and certain heirs — preserving the cap on appraised-value increases is a real-dollar issue. We coordinate with the homestead transfer rules during probate to preserve that exemption where possible.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months.

localFaqs.question: We're considering tearing down and rebuilding our Bellaire home. Does that affect estate planning?
localFaqs.answer: It can, in two ways. First, the basis in the property steps up at death — meaning a tear-down and rebuild that creates substantial appreciation may be more tax-efficient if held until death. Second, the homestead exemption and any over-65 cap reset on a major rebuild, so timing matters. We coordinate with your CPA on these issues during the planning conversation.

probate-attorney-west-university-tx.localContent:
West University Place — known to almost everyone simply as "West U" — is a small independent city of about 15,000 people, completely surrounded by the City of Houston and just south of Rice University and the Texas Medical Center. Incorporated in 1925, West U has consistently ranked among the most desirable residential addresses in Houston, with its tree-lined streets, walkable layout, and exceptional Houston ISD-zoned schools. Property values in West U are among the highest in the metro, and the homeowner base is heavy with physicians from the Medical Center, Rice and Baylor faculty, energy executives, and longtime West U families whose children grew up in the same neighborhood. That demographic mix means estate planning here is rarely simple — there's usually a high-value home, retirement and brokerage accounts, sometimes a vacation property elsewhere, and frequently a charitable component.

Like Bellaire, West U residents are sometimes surprised to find that their independent city doesn't have its own probate court — probate jurisdiction in Texas follows the county. West U is in Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. localFaqs.question: West U has its own city government — is probate handled by the city?
localFaqs.answer: No. West University Place is a separate municipality from Houston, but probate jurisdiction in Texas follows the county. localFaqs.question: I'm a physician at the Medical Center with substantial retirement assets. Should I use a trust?
localFaqs.answer: For many West U physicians, yes — though the reasons vary. A revocable living trust can keep your West U home and brokerage accounts out of probate (privacy is part of the appeal for high-profile professionals), coordinate beneficiary designations on multiple retirement plans, and address asset-protection concerns appropriately. Retirement accounts pass by beneficiary designation and don't need a trust to avoid probate, but the trust can serve as a contingent beneficiary for protection of minor or vulnerable heirs. We'll walk through whether one fits your situation honestly.

localFaqs.question: We have a vacation home in Colorado and a beach house in Florida. Do we need probates in three states?
localFaqs.answer: Without planning, yes — Texas probate for the West U home, Colorado ancillary probate for the mountain house, and Florida ancillary probate for the beach house. Three filings, three sets of attorney fees, three timelines. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state properties usually avoids the ancillary probates entirely. For West U families with multi-state real estate, this is one of the highest-value planning steps.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months. Trust administrations bypass court and run on the trustee's pace.

probate-attorney-meyerland-houston-tx.heroIntro: Meyerland is one of Houston's most established neighborhoods — anchored by a long-rooted Jewish community, an aging homeowner base, and the post-Harvey reality of a flood plain that has reshaped property and title across the area. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Meyerland families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-meyerland-houston-tx.localContent:
Meyerland sits on Houston's southwest side along Brays Bayou, developed in the 1950s by the Meyer family on what had been the Meyer ranch. The neighborhood has long been the heart of Houston's Jewish community, anchored by Beth Yeshurun, Beth Israel, and several other congregations along the Braeswood corridor, the Evelyn Rubenstein JCC, and a strong network of Jewish day schools and community organizations. Many Meyerland homeowners have been in the same house for 30, 40, or 50 years. The post-Harvey reality, however, has reshaped the neighborhood significantly — repeated flooding from Tropical Storm Allison, the Memorial Day floods, the Tax Day floods, and Hurricane Harvey forced thousands of Meyerland owners to either tear down and rebuild higher, sell to investor buyers, or repair and stay. That history affects probate here in ways it doesn't elsewhere — title gaps, insurance complications, contractor liens, and rebuild basis questions all show up in Meyerland estates.

Meyerland is in the City of Houston and Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown.

If you're administering a parent's Meyerland estate, dealing with a contested will, working through post-flood title issues, or putting a plan in place, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for paid-off Meyerland homes; particularly common for long-tenured owners
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will, or when post-flood ownership transitions left title gaps
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Title-Cleanup Heirship Proceedings — for properties where rebuilding, sales, or transfers happened without proper probate after a death

Estate Planning for Meyerland Families

For Meyerland's mix of long-tenured homeowners and post-Harvey rebuilders, planning needs to address both the home itself and the documentation behind it. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families and owners of multiple properties
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Meyerland home out of probate
  • Charitable giving aligned with synagogue and community foundation goals

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Meyerland residents?
localFaqs.answer: Meyerland is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — all at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing.

localFaqs.question: Our Meyerland house was rebuilt after Harvey but we never updated my mother's will or deed. Now she's passed. What now?
localFaqs.answer: This is one of the most common Meyerland probate scenarios. The will written before Harvey is still valid as long as it was properly executed; the rebuild simply changed the structure on the lot, not the underlying real property the will controls. The bigger issue is usually whether deed records, FEMA paperwork, contractor liens, and insurance proceeds were all handled cleanly during the rebuild. We work through the title chain to identify any gaps and clear them as part of probate.

localFaqs.question: We took an FEMA grant or buyout after a flood. How does that affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the program. A buyout typically transferred ownership of the lot to the city or county, so the lot itself is no longer part of the estate. An elevation grant or rebuild grant may have created secondary obligations on the title (deed restrictions, conditions on resale, etc.) that the executor needs to identify before selling or transferring. We pull the records and walk through the implications during probate.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. Estates with title-cleanup needs from past flood events can take longer because of the additional documentation required.

The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Braeswood families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-braeswood-houston-tx.localContent:
Braeswood Place runs along Brays Bayou between Stella Link and Buffalo Speedway, just east of Meyerland and west of the Texas Medical Center. The neighborhood — sometimes called "Braeswood," with the actual Braeswood Place homeowners' association comprising several smaller subdivisions — was developed primarily in the 1950s, and it shares some of Meyerland's character: a strong residential identity, long-tenured homeowners, and a deep connection to Houston's Jewish community along the Braeswood corridor. Like Meyerland, Braeswood was hit hard by Tropical Storm Allison, the Memorial Day flood, the Tax Day flood, and Hurricane Harvey. The result today is a neighborhood that mixes original 1950s and 1960s homes with high-end rebuilds, and the difference between a long-tenured estate and a recent investor flip can be significant for probate purposes.

Braeswood is in the City of Houston and Harris County, so probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Braeswood residents?
localFaqs.answer: Braeswood is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — all at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing.

localFaqs.question: We rebuilt our Braeswood house after Harvey. Will the rebuilt structure pass through probate the same way?
localFaqs.answer: Yes — the structure is part of the real property, and real property passes either by deed (joint survivorship, TOD deed, trust) or through probate based on how the deed is held. The rebuild itself doesn't change that. What can complicate things is the underlying paperwork: rebuild contracts, contractor liens, FEMA grant conditions, and insurance proceeds sometimes leave residual claims or restrictions on the title that an executor needs to address. We pull the title and work through any cleanup as part of probate.

localFaqs.question: My parents owned their Braeswood home outright. Is there a way to avoid probate entirely?
localFaqs.answer: Yes — for a paid-off Braeswood home, a Texas Transfer on Death Deed is usually the simplest route. Recorded with the Harris County Clerk, it transfers title to a named beneficiary at death without probate. A revocable living trust does the same job and offers more flexibility, but for a single home with simple heirs the TOD deed is typically the right tool. We can prepare and record one quickly.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months.

probate-attorney-needville-tx.heroIntro: Needville is a small, agricultural community in southern Fort Bend County — known for its cattle ranches, the annual Needville Harvest Festival, and a small-town character that has held on as the rest of the metro has grown around it. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Needville families through Fort Bend probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-needville-tx.localContent:
Needville sits in southern Fort Bend County, well south of Rosenberg, in some of the most agricultural country left in the Houston region. The community grew up around farming and ranching in the late 1800s, and it has held on to its rural character even as Sugar Land, Richmond, and Rosenberg have grown into full suburbs. Cattle ranches, hay fields, family farms, and the Needville ISD anchor the area. Many of our Needville clients are families who've owned land in the same place for three or four generations — sometimes longer. That depth of land tenure brings its own probate issues. Inherited land where probate was skipped at one or more deaths in the chain. Mineral interests held under property that has been farmed for a century. Equipment and livestock that need to keep working through any administration. These are different questions than the ones we field for high-rise condo owners in town.

Needville is in Fort Bend County, so probate matters are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond — about 30 minutes north. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Fort Bend's docket runs efficiently, and uncontested probate typically closes in 3 to 6 months.

If you're administering an estate, clearing title on inherited land, or putting a plan in place for a working farm or ranch, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Needville homes and farmland with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — frequently used for Needville family land where probate was never opened
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Heirship Title-Cleanup Proceedings — for land that has passed informally between generations

Estate Planning for Needville Families

For families with land, livestock, equipment, and sometimes mineral interests, planning is about more than a will. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for keeping family land out of probate and structuring how it passes to the next generation
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Needville home or family land out of probate
  • Land use entities (LLCs, partnerships) for multi-heir farm and ranch transitions
  • Mineral interest planning, where applicable

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Fort Bend County

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in Fort Bend County. Family land disputes are unfortunately common and rarely improve with delay — the two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Needville families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Needville and South Fort Bend County

Our office serves clients throughout Needville, Beasley, Kendleton, Rosenberg, Damon, Boling, Pleak, and surrounding rural Fort Bend communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is probate filed for a Needville resident?
localFaqs.answer: Needville is in Fort Bend County, so probate cases are filed with the Fort Bend County Clerk and heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Richmond is about a 30-minute drive north of Needville.

localFaqs.question: Our family has owned land in Needville for four generations and probate was never opened. Can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one for each generation. It's solvable, but it takes more work than a routine probate. We handle these cases for Fort Bend ranching and farming families regularly, and we can map out exactly what's required to clear title before any sale, lease, or family transfer.

localFaqs.question: We have a working cattle operation. Should the land be in a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes — particularly when more than one heir will inherit, or when the operation needs to continue uninterrupted after a death. A revocable living trust holding the land, equipment, and brand registrations can avoid probate entirely and keep the operation running through the transition. For larger family operations, an LLC or family partnership held by the trust can also help with management among multiple heirs. We'll walk through what fits.

localFaqs.question: We have mineral interests under our farmland. Do those go through probate the same way?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes — mineral interests are real property under Texas law, so they pass under the same rules as the surface estate. Where they're severed (separately deeded from the surface), they need to be addressed in their own right. Royalty payments often continue to operators that haven't been notified of the death, creating accounting issues for the executor. We identify and address mineral interests as part of probate for Needville and surrounding Fort Bend ranching families.

probate-attorney-pearland-tx.heroIntro: Pearland sits primarily in Brazoria County, with smaller portions in Harris and Fort Bend. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles Pearland probate matters in all three jurisdictions and has represented Brazoria County families for over 25 years.

Most of Pearland sits in Brazoria County, but a smaller portion of north Pearland is in Harris County, and a sliver to the west is in Fort Bend. For the Brazoria County majority of Pearland residents, probate matters are heard by Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court at the Brazoria County Courthouse, 111 East Locust Street in Angleton — about a 25-minute drive south of central Pearland. localFaqs.question: Pearland is in three counties — which one handles probate for my family member?

localFaqs.question: Where is the Brazoria County probate court?
localFaqs.answer: Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court is located at the Brazoria County Courthouse, 111 East Locust Street, Room 201, Angleton, TX 77515 — about a 25-minute drive south of central Pearland. Filings are made through the Brazoria County Clerk.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Brazoria County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Brazoria County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 3 months. Contested cases take longer.

localFaqs.question: We bought our Shadow Creek Ranch home with my parents on the deed — does it still need probate when they die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the form of co-ownership. If the deed includes "with right of survivorship" language, title passes to the surviving owners without probate. If the deed is just joint tenancy or tenancy in common with no survivorship language, the deceased parent's interest must pass through probate or by Muniment of Title. We can review the deed for you in a free consultation.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Silverlake residents?

localFaqs.question: My parents bought their Silverlake home in the 1990s. What's the simplest way to keep it out of probate?
localFaqs.answer: For a homestead in a single owner's name, the most cost-effective tool is usually a Texas Transfer on Death Deed. It's recorded with the Brazoria County Clerk during the owner's lifetime, names a beneficiary, and transfers title automatically at death without probate. It can be revoked or amended at any time. We prepare and record TOD deeds for Silverlake homeowners regularly and can usually do it in a single appointment.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Brazoria County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Brazoria County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — when the estate qualifies. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: Mom lived in Silverlake but had a vacation condo in Galveston. One probate or two?
localFaqs.answer: If both properties are in Texas and your mother died a Texas resident, one Brazoria County probate handles both — though we'll record certified orders in Galveston County to clear title on the condo. If a property is in another state, that state typically requires a separate "ancillary probate." A revocable living trust can avoid that second proceeding entirely if planning is done in advance.

Shadow Creek Ranch sprawls across more than 3,500 acres in west Pearland, anchored by Shadow Creek Parkway and the lakes that give the community its name. It's one of the largest master-planned communities in the southern half of the Houston metro, with thousands of homes, multiple schools in the Alvin and Fort Bend ISD footprints, and one of the most ethnically and professionally diverse populations of any Pearland neighborhood. We see a wide range of clients here — Texas Medical Center physicians and nurses, energy professionals, software workers, small-business owners — and that mix shapes the planning conversations. Shadow Creek Ranch sits in the Brazoria County portion of Pearland. localFaqs.question: Where do I file probate for a Shadow Creek Ranch resident?
localFaqs.answer: Shadow Creek Ranch is in the Brazoria County portion of Pearland, so probate is filed with the Brazoria County Clerk and heard by Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 in Angleton. The court phone is (979) 864-1622.

localFaqs.question: My parents have property in another country and a home here in Shadow Creek Ranch. How does that work?
localFaqs.answer: Foreign property is governed by the laws of the country where it sits — a Texas will doesn't directly transfer real estate in India, Mexico, Nigeria, the Philippines, or anywhere else outside the U.S. We typically recommend that clients with significant foreign assets coordinate a Texas estate plan with a separate plan or will in the country where the foreign property is located, often with help from local counsel. The Texas plan should clearly identify which assets it covers so there's no conflict.

localFaqs.question: We bought our Shadow Creek Ranch home with both spouses on the deed. Does it still need probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the deed. If the deed includes "with right of survivorship" language, title passes to the surviving spouse without probate. A standard Texas community-property deed without survivorship language does not — the deceased spouse's interest passes through their will or by intestate succession. We can review the deed in a free consultation and tell you exactly which path applies.

localFaqs.question: I'm an executor but I work full-time at the Texas Medical Center. How much time will probate take?
localFaqs.answer: For an uncontested independent administration, expect a few hours total over 3 to 6 months — most of it concentrated at the beginning when we open the case and at the end when we wind up. We handle the filings, court appearances, creditor notices, and inventory work; you sign documents and provide information when asked. The role is more demanding when the estate is contested or when an heir is uncooperative.

probate-attorney-friendswood-tx.heroIntro: Friendswood spans Galveston and Harris Counties — though most of the city sits in Galveston. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Friendswood families through both county probate courts for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-friendswood-tx.localContent:
Friendswood is a small, independent city tucked between Pearland, League City, and the Clear Lake area. Founded by Quakers in the 1890s, it has grown into one of the most affluent and family-oriented suburbs in the Houston metro, consistently ranked among the top school districts in Texas. The population is heavy on professional couples — engineers from the NASA / Johnson Space Center corridor, refinery and chemical-plant managers from the Bay Area industrial belt, and Texas Medical Center physicians who chose Friendswood for the schools. That demographic profile shapes the kind of estate planning we do for Friendswood clients: layered retirement accounts, employer pensions, life insurance, college savings for children, and increasingly, plans that contemplate caring for aging parents.

Friendswood is unusual in that it straddles two counties — most of the city is in Galveston County, with a smaller portion to the northwest in Harris County. Where probate is filed depends on the deceased's actual legal residence, not the ZIP code. For the Galveston County majority, probate matters are heard by Galveston County Probate Court at 600 59th Street in Galveston. For the Harris County portion, cases go to one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation.

If you've recently lost a family member, are serving as executor, or want to build out a plan that protects your home and family, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in the appropriate county
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Friendswood homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

For Friendswood's professional, family-focused population, planning is about more than a will. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament — including testamentary trusts for minor children
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families and clients with multi-state property
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds
  • Guardianship designations for minor children

localFaqs.question: Friendswood is in two counties — which one handles my family member's probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the deceased's legal residence at the time of death. Most of Friendswood is in Galveston County, with a smaller portion in Harris County. We confirm the correct county at the first consultation by checking the property records and the deceased's address. Filing in the wrong county wastes time and filing fees, so getting it right at the start matters.

localFaqs.question: My spouse worked at NASA. How is the federal pension handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: Federal pensions through FERS or CSRS pass by beneficiary designation rather than through probate. The surviving spouse is usually entitled to a survivor annuity if elected at retirement, plus any unpaid lump-sum amounts. The Office of Personnel Management handles the claim — not the probate court. We help Friendswood families coordinate the OPM claim with the rest of the estate, since federal pensions don't show up in the probate inventory but absolutely belong on the estate-planning checklist.

localFaqs.question: We have young children. What happens to them if both parents die?
localFaqs.answer: If you don't name a guardian in your will, the court chooses — and the court's choice may not be the person you'd pick. A Texas will should name both a guardian (the person who raises the children) and a separate trustee (the person who manages money on their behalf), since those are different jobs and not always the same person. We draft these provisions for Friendswood families regularly and can usually complete a simple plan in two appointments.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Galveston County versus Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months. Harris County typically runs 4 to 8 months — the four probate courts there carry significantly more volume. The county is determined by the deceased's residence, not your preference, so the timeline follows automatically.

localFaqs.question: Our family has had Manvel land for three generations and probate was never done. Can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one for each generation. It takes more work than a routine administration, but it's solvable. We handle these cases for Brazoria County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: We own mineral rights under our Manvel acreage. Do they go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Yes — mineral rights are real property under Texas law and pass through probate the same way the surface estate does, unless they've been deeded out separately or covered by a Transfer on Death Deed. Producing royalties also have an income stream that needs to be redirected to the heirs once title is cleared. We coordinate the title work with the operator's division-order department to keep royalties flowing through the transition.

localFaqs.question: We just moved into a Pomona house — do we need an estate plan already?

Updating beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and insurance is part of the same conversation.

localFaqs.answer: Iowa Colony is in Brazoria County, so probate is filed with the Brazoria County Clerk and heard by Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court at 111 East Locust Street in Angleton. The court phone is (979) 864-1622.

localFaqs.question: We inherited family acreage along 288 — can we use a Small Estate Affidavit?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no. Small Estate Affidavits in Texas only transfer non-real-property assets — bank accounts, vehicles, personal property — up to the $75,000 statutory threshold. They do not transfer real estate, including agricultural land. For inherited acreage, you'll usually need either a Determination of Heirship (if there was no will), a Muniment of Title (if there's a will and no unpaid debts other than a mortgage), or a full probate. We can review your situation and recommend the most efficient option.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Brazoria County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Brazoria County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — when the estate qualifies. Contested cases take longer.

localFaqs.question: We just bought in Sierra Vista. What's the bare minimum estate planning we should do?
localFaqs.answer: For a new homeowner with minor children, the basics are: a will (naming a guardian for the children and a trustee for any inheritance), durable power of attorney, medical power of attorney, and HIPAA authorization. A Transfer on Death Deed for the home is often added so the house bypasses probate entirely. We can usually complete that core package in two appointments — and updating the beneficiary designations on your retirement accounts and life insurance is part of the same conversation.

probate-attorney-alvin-tx.heroIntro: Alvin is a small Brazoria County town with deep agricultural roots — and the hometown of Hall of Fame pitcher Nolan Ryan. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Alvin families through Brazoria County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-alvin-tx.localContent:
Alvin sits in central Brazoria County, about 20 miles south of Houston, anchored by Highway 35 and the Alvin Independent School District. It's an older Texas town — incorporated in the 1890s — with agricultural roots that still show up in the cattle operations, hay fields, and family farms scattered around the city limits. The community is best known nationally as the hometown of Nolan Ryan, but locally it's defined by long-tenured families, working-class neighborhoods, and a steady population that has lived in the area for two or three generations. That demographic profile shapes the kind of probate work we do for Alvin clients. Inherited land, multi-generational title issues, and modest estates that benefit from streamlined tools — Small Estate Affidavits, Muniments of Title — come up frequently.

Alvin sits in Brazoria County. Probate matters are heard by Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court at 111 East Locust Street in Angleton, about 20 minutes south of Alvin. Filings go through the Brazoria County Clerk, and uncontested matters typically close in 3 to 6 months.

If you've recently lost a family member, are clearing title to land that has been in the family for generations, or want to put a simple plan in place to protect your home, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review. We'll tell you honestly whether you need a lawyer at all.

Probate Services for Alvin Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Brazoria County:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Brazoria County
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Alvin homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — common for longtime Alvin families with land that was passed informally
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the $75,000 statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Alvin Families

For most Alvin households, a simple, well-built plan handles everything that's needed. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — a low-cost way to keep your Alvin home out of probate
  • Revocable Living Trusts, where complexity warrants — for example, multiple parcels of family land or a blended family

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Alvin beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

localFaqs.question: Where do I file probate for an Alvin resident?
localFaqs.answer: Alvin is in Brazoria County. Probate is filed with the Brazoria County Clerk and heard by Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court at 111 East Locust Street in Angleton. The court phone is (979) 864-1622.

localFaqs.question: My father's estate is small — just a paid-off house and a bank account. Do we really need probate?
localFaqs.answer: Maybe not in the formal sense. If the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage and there is a valid will, a Muniment of Title is often the right tool — it's faster and less expensive than full administration. For non-real-property assets under $75,000 (and no real estate other than a homestead in some cases), a Small Estate Affidavit may work. We'll review the assets at a free consultation and recommend the simplest path that actually clears title.

localFaqs.question: Our family has Alvin land that's been passed down without paperwork for 50 years. Can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title problems caused by deaths that were never probated are resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one per generation. It takes more work than a routine administration, but it's solvable. We handle these cases for Brazoria County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: How do I keep my Alvin home out of probate without paying for a trust?
localFaqs.answer: For most homeowners, a Texas Transfer on Death Deed is the right answer. It's recorded with the Brazoria County Clerk during your lifetime, names a beneficiary, and transfers the home automatically at death without probate. It's much less expensive than a revocable living trust, can be revoked or amended at any time, and works for the typical situation where the home is the primary asset. We can prepare and record one for you in a single appointment.

localFaqs.question: We're a young family in Meridiana. What's the most important piece of estate planning?
localFaqs.answer: For families with minor children, naming a guardian and a trustee in a Texas will is usually the most consequential decision. The guardian raises the children if both parents die; the trustee manages money on the children's behalf — often a different person, with a different skill set. Without a will, a Texas court chooses, and the result may not match what you'd pick. The second most consequential decision is reviewing beneficiary designations on 401(k)s, IRAs, and life insurance — those bypass the will entirely.

localFaqs.question: I'm helping with my mother's estate — she lived in another state but owned the Meridiana lot we're building on. What now?
localFaqs.answer: If the primary probate is happening in another state, the Texas property typically requires a separate "ancillary probate" in Brazoria County to clear title. We routinely represent out-of-state heirs and can serve as Texas registered agent, file the ancillary proceeding, and coordinate with your home-state attorney. If your mother had a revocable living trust holding the lot, that ancillary probate is often avoidable.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Brazoria County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Brazoria County typically close in 3 to 6 months. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: Do I need to attend court in person if probate is filed in Angleton?
localFaqs.answer: For an uncontested probate, generally one initial hearing is required where the executor or applicant appears in person to be sworn in and qualified. Subsequent matters are often handled by filings or, in some cases, by Zoom. Brazoria County's court is more accessible than the Harris County probate courts because the docket carries less volume and hearings are typically scheduled more quickly.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Brazoria County?

localFaqs.question: I work for the county and have a TCDRS pension. Does it pass through probate?
localFaqs.answer: The Texas County and District Retirement System pays benefits to the beneficiary you designated with TCDRS, not through your will. That means TCDRS benefits bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. Problems arise when the beneficiary is an ex-spouse, has predeceased, or was never updated. Reviewing your TCDRS designation alongside your will is one of the simplest pieces of estate planning we do for Angleton county-employee clients.

localFaqs.question: My spouse worked at Dow for 35 years. How are the pension and 401(k) handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: Defined-benefit pensions usually continue paying a survivor benefit to the spouse if a joint-and-survivor option was elected at retirement. The 401(k) passes by beneficiary designation directly to whoever is named — bypassing probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is in place. Both are administered by the plan, not by the probate court. Reviewing the elections and the named beneficiaries is one of the most important pieces of estate planning we do for Dow families.

localFaqs.question: My father was part of an asbestos settlement that's still paying. What happens at his death?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the trust or settlement structure. Many asbestos and chemical-exposure trusts continue paying scheduled installments after the worker's death — but the payments shift to the estate or to a designated beneficiary, and the heirs need to provide the trust administrator with proof of death and proof of executor authority. We coordinate with the trust administrator on Lake Jackson families' behalf so payments don't stop unnecessarily during probate.

localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Brazoria County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — when the estate qualifies. Contested cases take significantly longer.
