localFaqs.answer: Harris County has four statutory probate courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — and your case is randomly assigned at filing. localFaqs.question: My spouse was a federal employee at NASA. How does the federal pension and TSP work?
localFaqs.answer: FERS pensions pay a survivor annuity to the surviving spouse if a survivor election was made at retirement — that benefit is administered by the U.S. Office of Personnel Management, not the probate court. The Thrift Savings Plan passes by beneficiary designation, also bypassing probate. FEGLI life insurance similarly passes by beneficiary designation. Reviewing those federal designations alongside your Texas will is one of the most important pieces of estate planning we do for Clear Lake federal-employee families — old elections that name an ex-spouse or a deceased parent are a common, fixable problem.

localFaqs.question: We have a boat slipped at Watergate Yachting Center. Does the boat go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Boats are titled in Texas through the Department of Motor Vehicles (or Texas Parks and Wildlife for certain vessels), so transfer at death depends on how the title is held. A boat held jointly with right of survivorship transfers automatically; one held solely in the deceased's name passes through probate or, where eligible, by Small Estate Affidavit. Marina slip leases and dry-storage agreements have their own transfer rules through the contract — we coordinate with the marina as part of administering the estate.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months, when the estate qualifies.

localFaqs.question: We have a vacation home in Galveston and primary home in League City. Two probates?
localFaqs.answer: If both properties are in Galveston County and you die a Texas resident, one probate handles both. If your vacation property is in another state — say, a beach house in Florida — that property typically triggers a separate "ancillary probate" there, on top of the Texas probate. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that.

probate-attorney-webster-tx.heroIntro: Webster is a small Harris County city in the heart of the Bay Area aerospace and retail corridor — home to Baybrook Mall and a workforce drawn from NASA and the Texas Medical Center's southern campuses. The population reflects that mix: aerospace engineers, medical professionals, and retail managers all live within a few miles of each other, often in townhomes, condominiums, and apartment communities rather than the single-family neighborhoods that dominate the rest of the Bay Area.

localFaqs.question: Which probate court will hear my case if I live in Webster?
localFaqs.answer: Webster is in Harris County, so probate cases are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. We handle filings in all four courts regularly.

localFaqs.question: I'm a nurse at Houston Methodist Clear Lake. How is my hospital pension handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: Hospital retirement plans — usually a 403(b) for nonprofit hospitals — pass by beneficiary designation, bypassing probate entirely. Defined-benefit pensions, where they still exist, generally pay a survivor annuity to a spouse if elected at retirement. Group life insurance through the employer also passes by beneficiary designation. Reviewing all three together — and updating designations after a marriage, divorce, or birth — is one of the simplest and highest-value pieces of estate planning we do.

localFaqs.question: I own a condo in Webster — does it need to go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on how title is held. A condo in joint ownership with right of survivorship passes outside probate. A condo solely in the deceased's name with no Transfer on Death Deed will need probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding. A recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed transfers the condo automatically at death without probate. Reviewing your deed now is one of the simplest steps in estate planning.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months when the estate qualifies.

Today, the population is a mix of the original generation (now in their 70s, 80s, and 90s, often surviving spouses) and a younger wave of professionals — engineers, pilots, defense contractors, and Bay Area medical professionals — who chose the community for its waterfront access to Clear Lake, the established neighborhoods, and the mid-century homes that have been steadily renovated over the past two decades.

localFaqs.question: My late husband was a long-retired NASA civil servant. What do I do about his CSRS pension?
localFaqs.answer: CSRS survivor annuities are administered by the U.S. Office of Personnel Management. The surviving spouse files a claim with OPM, supported by a marriage certificate and the death certificate; if a survivor election was made at retirement, the annuity continues for the surviving spouse's lifetime. We help Nassau Bay surviving spouses file the OPM claim and coordinate it with the rest of the estate, since the federal benefit is separate from anything the probate court sees.

localFaqs.question: We've owned our Nassau Bay home since 1972. Should we put it in a trust now?
localFaqs.answer: Often, no — a Texas Transfer on Death Deed is simpler and less expensive for a homestead held in your own names, and it accomplishes the same goal of keeping the home out of probate. A revocable living trust makes more sense when there's significant out-of-state property, a complex blended family, or a desire to control the home's use after death. We'll review your specific situation in a free consultation and recommend the simpler path when it works.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?

Moore, PLLC has represented Seabrook families and waterfront property owners through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-seabrook-tx.localContent:
Seabrook sits where Clear Lake meets Galveston Bay, just across the channel from Kemah. The city is defined by water — the marinas along NASA Parkway, the commercial fishing fleet at the Old Seabrook waterfront, the sailing community at Watergate Yachting Center, and the waterfront homes lining Todville Road and the bay's northern shore. The population mixes longtime residents, retirees who moved to the bay for the boating, and aerospace and Bay Area medical professionals who chose Seabrook for the water access. That gives Seabrook estates a recognizable signature: boats, marina slips, waterfront property, and often a vacation or second home somewhere on the coast.

Boats are real assets that transfer in real ways. Texas titles vessels through the Department of Motor Vehicles or Texas Parks and Wildlife depending on the type, and a boat owned solely by the deceased usually passes through probate or by Small Estate Affidavit if eligible. Marina slip leases have their own transfer rules through the marina contract. We see all of these regularly in Seabrook estates and coordinate with marinas, brokers, and TPWD to keep transfers moving.

Seabrook sits in Harris County. Probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, with cases randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: My father owned a sailboat at Watergate. How do we transfer it after his death?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on how the boat was titled. A boat held jointly with right of survivorship passes automatically to the surviving owner without probate. A boat solely in the deceased's name passes through the will (in probate) or by Small Estate Affidavit if the estate qualifies under the $75,000 threshold and the boat is the only major non-real-property asset. We coordinate the title transfer with TPWD or the Texas DMV depending on the vessel type, and we handle the marina slip transfer with Watergate or the relevant marina at the same time.

localFaqs.question: We have a fishing camp in another state plus our Seabrook home. Two probates?
localFaqs.answer: Real property in another state typically requires a separate "ancillary probate" in that state if the camp is titled in the deceased's individual name. That means two probates running side by side. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids the second proceeding entirely. We can help you decide whether the trust is worth setting up given your particular fishing camp and the laws of the state it sits in.

The Kemah Boardwalk has anchored the town's identity for the last quarter-century, and the surrounding waterfront has filled in with marinas, restaurants, vacation rentals, and a steady supply of waterfront homes and condominiums. Each of those groups brings a different probate profile to our office.

Two patterns come up often. First, vacation homes and second properties — when the deceased lived in Houston, in another county, or even in another state, the Kemah property still has to clear title, and how that happens depends on where the primary probate is taking place. Second, boats — Kemah is a boating town, and titled vessels usually have to be addressed alongside the home as part of the same estate. We handle both regularly.

Kemah sits in Galveston County. Probate matters are heard by Galveston County Probate Court at 600 59th Street in Galveston, about 30 minutes south. localFaqs.question: We own a Kemah weekend condo but live in Houston full-time. How does title transfer at death?
localFaqs.answer: If you die a Texas resident, your Houston-area Harris County probate handles the Kemah condo too — but we'll record certified orders in Galveston County to clear title on the condo. If you've recorded a Texas Transfer on Death Deed for the condo, it transfers automatically at death without any probate touching it. For weekend or vacation properties held in one spouse's name, the TOD deed is often the cleanest tool.

localFaqs.question: My father lived in another state but owned a slip and a boat at the Kemah Boardwalk. What now?
localFaqs.answer: If his primary probate is happening in another state, the Texas property — including the slip lease and the boat — typically requires a separate "ancillary probate" in Galveston County to transfer cleanly. We routinely represent out-of-state heirs in Galveston ancillary proceedings, coordinate the title transfer for the boat with TPWD or the Texas DMV, and handle the slip-lease transfer with the marina. If your father had a revocable living trust holding the property, the ancillary probate is often avoidable.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Galveston County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 3 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: My grandmother's house has been in the family for 40 years and she died without a will decades ago. Can we still get title in our names?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. When a Texas property owner dies without a will and probate was never opened, title can be cleared through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one for each generation. It takes more work than a routine administration, but it's solvable. We handle these cases for Dickinson families regularly.

localFaqs.question: Do I really need a lawyer if Dad's estate is just a small bank account and a paid-off home?
localFaqs.answer: Maybe not for a full probate, but you'll likely need either a Muniment of Title (if there's a will and no unpaid debts other than a mortgage) or a Small Estate Affidavit (for non-real-property assets under $75,000 — the home is typically excluded but in some narrow cases qualifies). Both are simpler and less expensive than a full administration. We'll review the assets at a free consultation and recommend the simplest path that actually clears title.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Galveston County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months. Small Estate Affidavits are faster still, often resolved in a few weeks once the proper documentation is filed.

probate-attorney-texas-city-tx.heroIntro: Texas City is a Galveston County industrial hub anchored by the Marathon and Valero refineries — a community shaped by a century of petrochemical work. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Texas City families through Galveston County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-texas-city-tx.localContent:
Texas City sits on the western shore of Galveston Bay, defined for over a century by the refineries and petrochemical plants along its industrial waterfront — Marathon Petroleum, Valero, INEOS, Eastman, and the Port of Texas City. The city's identity is inseparable from that industrial economy, and its history includes the 1947 Texas City Disaster, still one of the worst industrial accidents in U.S. history. The population today is heavily blue-collar, with multi-generational refinery and plant families, union pensioners, and a working professional class. That demographic shapes the kind of estate planning we do for Texas City clients. Layered employer benefits — defined-benefit pensions for older workers, 401(k)s and 457(b)s for younger workers, employer life insurance, supplemental disability — show up across most working-family estates.

We also see asbestos and chemical-exposure litigation regularly. Many Texas City refinery and plant workers have settlements or trust claims related to long-tail occupational exposure, and those payments may continue after the worker's death — but only if the heirs know about the claim and follow up with the trust administrator. We handle that coordination as part of administering Texas City estates.

Texas City is in Galveston County. Probate matters are heard by Galveston County Probate Court at 600 59th Street in Galveston, about 15 minutes south. Filings are made through the Galveston County Clerk, and uncontested matters typically close in 3 to 6 months. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Galveston County
  • Muniment of Title — when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will

  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families and multi-state property
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Texas City home out of probate

localFaqs.question: My father retired from Marathon with a pension and a 401(k). How are those handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: Defined-benefit pensions usually continue paying a survivor annuity to the spouse if a joint-and-survivor option was elected at retirement. The 401(k) passes by beneficiary designation directly to whoever is named — bypassing probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is in place. Both are administered by the plan, not by the probate court. The biggest single mistake we see in refinery-family estates is an outdated beneficiary designation — naming a long-divorced spouse, a deceased parent, or no one at all. Reviewing those designations alongside the will is one of the highest-value planning steps a refinery family can take.

localFaqs.question: My uncle had an asbestos settlement that pays him quarterly. Does it stop when he dies?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the trust or settlement structure. Many asbestos and chemical-exposure trusts continue paying scheduled installments after the worker's death — but the payments shift to the estate or to a designated beneficiary, and the heirs need to provide the trust administrator with proof of death and proof of executor authority. Many heirs miss out simply because they don't know the claim exists. We coordinate with the trust administrator on Texas City families' behalf so payments don't stop unnecessarily.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Galveston County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — when the estate qualifies. Small Estate Affidavits, where eligible, are typically resolved in a few weeks.

probate-attorney-la-porte-tx.heroIntro: La Porte is a Harris County bay-side city — home to the San Jacinto Battleground and a workforce drawn from the surrounding refinery and petrochemical corridor. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented La Porte families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-la-porte-tx.localContent:
La Porte sits on the north shore of Galveston Bay, just east of the San Jacinto Monument and the San Jacinto Battleground State Historic Site. The city has been shaped by two distinct forces. To the west and south, the petrochemical and refining corridor — Houston Ship Channel facilities, Bayport Industrial Complex, and a string of major operators including Air Liquide, INEOS, LyondellBasell, and Albemarle — drives much of the local employment. To the east, the bay supports a fishing and recreational community along Sylvan Beach and the lower-bay neighborhoods. The result is a working-class to working-professional population with a recognizable mix of refinery employment, union ties, and waterfront leisure.

That demographic shapes the probate work we do for La Porte families. Layered employer benefits — defined-benefit pensions for older workers, 401(k)s for younger workers, group life and supplemental disability — show up across most estates. So do boats, occasional waterfront properties, and a steady volume of asbestos and chemical-exposure trust claims tied to long careers in the petrochemical industry. We see all of these regularly and handle the coordination as part of administering the estate.

La Porte sits in Harris County. Probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, with cases randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for La Porte Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in the Harris County Probate Courts:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for La Porte Industrial Families

For refinery, plant, and Ship Channel workers, careful coordination of pension, 401(k), and beneficiary designations is the highest-value planning step. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families and multi-state property
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your La Porte home out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for 401(k)s, pensions, union benefits, and life insurance
  • Coordination with asbestos or chemical-exposure trusts that may continue paying after death

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for La Porte families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving La Porte and the Ship Channel Communities

Our office serves clients throughout La Porte, Morgan's Point, Shoreacres, Pasadena, Deer Park, Bayport, Seabrook, Baytown, and surrounding Ship Channel communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Which probate court hears La Porte cases?
localFaqs.answer: La Porte is in Harris County. Probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing among Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4.

localFaqs.question: My husband worked at a Ship Channel plant for 30 years and was a union member. How are union benefits handled at death?
localFaqs.answer: Union pension and welfare-fund benefits are usually administered by the local's trust funds, not the probate court. Survivor benefits (pension annuity, group life insurance, sometimes supplemental death benefits) are paid based on plan rules and beneficiary designations. The surviving spouse files a claim with the fund directly. We help La Porte families coordinate the union claim with the rest of the estate, since union benefits don't show up in the probate inventory but absolutely belong on the planning checklist.

localFaqs.question: My father had a chemical-exposure settlement still paying. What happens at his death?
localFaqs.answer: Many asbestos and chemical-exposure trusts continue paying scheduled installments after the worker's death, with payments shifting to the estate or to a designated beneficiary. The heirs must provide the trust administrator with proof of death and proof of executor authority before payments resume. We coordinate with the trust administrator on La Porte families' behalf so payments don't lapse during probate.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months when the estate qualifies.

probate-attorney-pasadena-tx.heroIntro: Pasadena is the second-largest city in Harris County — a diverse, predominantly Latino community shaped by the Ship Channel refinery economy. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Pasadena families through Harris County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-pasadena-tx.localContent:
Pasadena is one of the largest cities in the Houston metro and the second-most populous city in Harris County. The community runs along the south shore of the Houston Ship Channel, defined for nearly a century by the refineries, petrochemical plants, and supporting industries that line Highway 225. Today Pasadena is a Latino-majority city with a deep multi-generational presence — many families have lived in Pasadena across three or four generations, with parents and grandparents who worked in the same refineries that employ the next generation. That continuity shapes the kind of estate planning and probate work we see. Modest estates that benefit from streamlined tools, layered employer pension and 401(k) benefits, and multi-generational title issues on family homes all show up regularly.

We handle a steady volume of Pasadena probate work for Spanish-speaking families, and we coordinate with Spanish-speaking family members during the process. We also see a recognizable pattern of cases where a parent or grandparent died years or decades ago without a probate, and the family now needs to clear title before a sale or refinance. These are solvable, and we handle them regularly.

Pasadena sits in Harris County. Probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, with cases randomly assigned at filing. The drive from most of Pasadena is 20 to 30 minutes. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Pasadena homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — common for longtime Pasadena families with homes that have been passed informally between generations

  • Beneficiary designation review for 401(k)s, pensions, union benefits, and life insurance
  • Coordination with asbestos or chemical-exposure trusts that may continue paying after death

Serving Pasadena and the Ship Channel Communities

localFaqs.question: Where do I file probate for a Pasadena resident?
localFaqs.answer: Pasadena is in Harris County. Probate is heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing among Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4.

localFaqs.question: My grandparents bought their Pasadena home in the 1960s and nobody ever did anything legal when my grandfather died. Can we still get clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one for each generation. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it is solvable. We handle these cases for Pasadena families regularly, and the process is the same regardless of how long ago the deaths occurred.

localFaqs.question: Some of my mother's heirs live in Mexico. Does that complicate the probate?
localFaqs.answer: It adds steps but it is not a barrier. Heirs who live abroad can sign documents at a U.S. consulate or before a notary in their home country, and we can serve notice through international mail or by other means the court approves. We've handled many Pasadena-area probates with heirs in Mexico, Central America, and beyond, and we coordinate the international notice and signature requirements as part of the case.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months when the estate qualifies. Heirship proceedings take longer, particularly when multiple generations need to be addressed.

probate-attorney-deer-park-tx.localContent:
Deer Park sits along the Houston Ship Channel between Pasadena and La Porte, anchored by Shell Deer Park (now operating in part as Pemex) and the Chevron Phillips Cedar Bayou facility. The city is one of the most identifiable refinery towns in Texas — the petrochemical complex employs thousands of Deer Park residents directly and supports much of the local service economy. The community is tight-knit, with a strong school district, multi-generational refinery families, and a population that often spans grandparents, parents, and adult children all working at the same complex. That continuity shapes the kind of estate planning we do for Deer Park clients.

The dominant planning theme for Deer Park families is layered employer compensation. Older workers and retirees often have defined-benefit pensions, group life insurance, post-retirement medical, and supplemental disability. Younger workers have 401(k)s, employer stock, and group coverage. Beneficiary designations across all of those products need to be coordinated with the will — and the single most common mistake we see is an old designation that names an ex-spouse or a deceased parent. Catching that during a planning review can save the family enormous trouble.

Deer Park sits in Harris County. Probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston. Cases are randomly assigned at filing. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: My father retired from Shell with a pension. Can my mother still receive payments after he dies?
localFaqs.answer: Usually yes, if a joint-and-survivor option was elected at retirement. Under that election, the pension continues paying a reduced amount to the surviving spouse for her lifetime — administered by Shell's plan administrator, not the probate court. The surviving spouse files a claim with the plan supported by the death certificate and marriage certificate. We help Deer Park families coordinate the plan claim with the rest of the estate.

localFaqs.question: I'm a Shell employee with stock and a 401(k). Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: The 401(k) passes by beneficiary designation directly to whoever you've named — bypassing probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is in place. Employer stock held in a brokerage account follows the same rule if there's a beneficiary designation; if not, it goes through probate. Reviewing both designations is one of the simplest pieces of estate planning we do for Deer Park refinery employees.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months when the estate qualifies.

probate-attorney-galveston-tx.localContent:
Galveston is unlike any other community in the Houston metro. The island has historic homes in the East End Historic District, beachfront condominiums along Seawall Boulevard, working-class neighborhoods, vacation rentals owned by people who live somewhere else entirely, and a tourism economy that brings the population up dramatically on summer weekends. Each of those housing patterns shapes the kind of probate work we do for Galveston clients. We see beneficiaries inheriting beach condos they don't want to keep, families clearing title on hurricane-damaged property that's been in the family for generations, and out-of-state heirs trying to administer an estate they've never set foot in.

The advantage for Galveston residents is that the courthouse is right on the island. Galveston County Probate Court sits at 600 59th Street, Suite 2305 in Galveston, in the Galveston County Courthouse. The court hears probate, guardianship, and mental health matters for the entire county — covering Galveston, League City, Friendswood, Texas City, Dickinson, Kemah, La Marque, and the surrounding Bay Area.

If you've inherited property on the island, are administering an estate, or want to put a plan in place that protects your home from lengthy court delays, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Galveston homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — common for older Galveston families with property whose chain of title was never cleared after a death

Many Galveston homeowners — especially beachfront condo owners — live somewhere else most of the year. A solid plan ties Texas property to your overall estate plan. We help with:

localFaqs.question: Where is the Galveston County Probate Court?
localFaqs.answer: Galveston County Probate Court is located at 600 59th Street, Suite 2305, Galveston, TX 77551, on the second-floor of the Galveston County Courthouse. The court phone is (409) 766-2251.

localFaqs.question: I live out of state but inherited a beach condo on the island — what do I do?
localFaqs.answer: If your relative's primary probate is happening in another state, the Texas property typically requires a separate "ancillary probate" in Galveston County to transfer title. We routinely represent out-of-state heirs and can serve as Texas registered agent, file the ancillary proceeding, and coordinate with your home-state attorney. If the relative had a revocable living trust holding the condo, that ancillary probate is often avoidable.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Galveston County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — if the estate qualifies.

localFaqs.question: Hurricane Ike damaged our family home decades ago and we never cleared title after my grandfather died. Can we still fix it?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship and, if needed, a series of heirship proceedings going back through the chain of title. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable. We handle these cases for Galveston families regularly.

La Marque sits in Galveston County. Probate matters are heard by Galveston County Probate Court at 600 59th Street in Galveston, about 20 minutes south. Filings are made through the Galveston County Clerk, and uncontested matters typically close in 3 to 6 months.

localFaqs.question: Can I just sign over my house to my son to avoid probate?
localFaqs.answer: You can, but it's almost always a bad idea. A lifetime gift of your home transfers your tax basis to your son, which can create a large capital gains bill when he eventually sells. It also exposes the home to your son's creditors, divorce, or financial trouble while you're still alive, and it can disqualify you from Medicaid long-term care if needed within five years. A Texas Transfer on Death Deed accomplishes the goal of avoiding probate without any of those downsides — your son gets a stepped-up tax basis at your death, and the home stays protected during your lifetime. We prepare and record TOD deeds for La Marque homeowners regularly.

localFaqs.question: My grandmother's home in La Marque has been in the family for 50 years and probate was never done. Can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. When a Texas homeowner dies without probate, title can be cleared through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed, one for each generation. It takes more work than a routine administration, but it's solvable. We handle these cases for La Marque families regularly.

localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — when the estate qualifies. Small Estate Affidavits, where eligible, are often resolved in a few weeks once the proper documentation is filed.

Built around the Goose Creek oil field discovery in 1916 and consolidated from several smaller towns in 1948, Baytown has long been defined by the petrochemical industry — ExxonMobil's Baytown refinery and chemical complex is one of the largest in the world and remains a major employer. That gives the city a deep working-class character with strong multi-generational ties: many Baytown families have lived in the same neighborhoods for two or three generations, often with parents and adult children employed in the same industry. We see a particular probate profile here. Estates frequently include 401(k)s, pensions, and retiree health benefits from ExxonMobil or one of the other major operators, modest but appreciated homes, and sometimes inherited property where chain of title was never formally cleared.

Most of Baytown is in Harris County, with small portions in Chambers County. Probate jurisdiction follows the deceased's actual physical address — a Baytown ZIP code alone doesn't tell you which county a particular home is in, and we confirm at the first consultation. For Harris County Baytown residents, probate matters are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston.

Whether you're administering a parent's estate, dealing with retiree benefits from a major employer, or putting a plan in place, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: Baytown is partly in Chambers County. How do I know which county handles probate?
localFaqs.answer: Probate jurisdiction follows the deceased's actual legal residence, not the ZIP code or mailing address. Most of Baytown sits in Harris County, but some neighborhoods east of the Cedar Bayou area are in Chambers County. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation by checking the property records. Filing in the wrong county wastes time and fees.

localFaqs.question: My dad worked at the Baytown refinery for 35 years. He had a pension and a 401(k). Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no. Pensions and 401(k)s pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning they bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. Problems arise when the named beneficiary is an ex-spouse, has predeceased, or was never updated after a major life change. Reviewing your beneficiary designations is one of the simplest and highest-value pieces of estate planning we do for Baytown industry families.

localFaqs.question: Our family home in Baytown has been in the family since the 1950s and we never probated my grandparents' estate. Is that fixable?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is required. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for older Baytown families regularly.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Harris County typically close in 4 to 8 months from filing, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. Chambers County, where some Baytown residents live, often moves faster — 3 to 5 months for uncontested matters.

probate-attorney-channelview-tx.heroIntro: Channelview is an industrial east Harris County community, anchored along the Houston Ship Channel and the I-10 corridor. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles Harris County probate for Channelview families with over 25 years of Texas experience.
probate-attorney-channelview-tx.localContent:
Channelview sits along the north side of the Houston Ship Channel between Houston proper and Baytown, anchored by I-10 and surrounded by the petrochemical and shipping industries that define east Harris County. It's a working-class community with deep roots — many Channelview families have been here for two or three generations, often with multiple family members employed at one of the area refineries, terminals, or shipping operations. The housing stock is mostly modest single-family homes from the 1950s through the 1980s, with longer-than-average tenure: residents tend to stay. Our probate work in Channelview reflects that demographic. localFaqs.question: My grandparents bought our Channelview house in the 1960s and we never did probate when they passed. Now my mom needs to sell. Is that fixable?
localFaqs.answer: Yes. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for longtime east Harris County families regularly. We can usually map out exactly what's required after reviewing the deed.

localFaqs.question: Dad's life insurance company is refusing to pay because of the contestability period. What can we do?
localFaqs.answer: The two-year contestability period after a policy is issued lets insurers investigate misrepresentations on the application — but it doesn't allow them to deny based on whatever they want. We pursue these claims regularly, often on contingency, and many denials are overturned when the actual application and medical records don't support the insurer's stated grounds. Don't accept the denial without having someone review the file.

localFaqs.question: I work for a petrochemical company and have employer stock plus a 401(k). Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: 401(k)s and most retirement accounts pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning they bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. Employer stock held in a brokerage account follows the same rule if there's a beneficiary designation; if it's held without one, it goes through probate. Reviewing your beneficiary designations is one of the simplest and highest-value pieces of estate planning we do for Chambers County industry professionals.

localFaqs.question: We have land that's been in our family for generations — is probate different?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes. Long-held Chambers County family land where successive owners died without probating their wills sometimes requires multiple Determinations of Heirship to clear title — one for each generation that didn't go through probate. It's solvable, but it takes more work than a routine administration. We handle these heirship cases for Chambers County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: Our family home was bought by my grandfather in the 1960s. The deed still has his name on it. How do we fix that?
localFaqs.answer: Through a Determination of Heirship — sometimes a series of them when multiple generations died without probate. It's more work than a single routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for east Harris County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: I'm worried about probate being expensive. Are there low-cost options?
localFaqs.answer: Yes. For modest estates, the Small Estate Affidavit (for qualifying estates under $75,000 in non-real-property assets) and the Muniment of Title proceeding (when there are no unpaid debts other than a mortgage) are both lower-cost alternatives to a full administration. And for planning ahead, a recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed costs a small fraction of probate and keeps the home out of court entirely. We can review your situation and tell you the lowest-cost option that actually fits.

probate-attorney-montgomery-tx.localContent:
Montgomery the city is one of the more historic small towns in southeast Texas. Founded in 1837, it's officially recognized as the "Birthplace of the Texas Flag" — the Lone Star Flag was designed there by Charles B. Stewart — and the historic downtown still reflects that 19th-century character. The city sits on the west side of Lake Conroe, along Highway 105, and the surrounding area has grown rapidly with lakefront development, master-planned communities like Bentwater and Walden, and a substantial population of retirees who chose the area for the lake and the lower cost of living. One source of confusion worth flagging: although the city is named Montgomery, the county seat — and the location of the only probate court in Montgomery County — is in Conroe, not in Montgomery the city. Probate filings for residents of the city of Montgomery are made at Conroe.

Probate matters for Montgomery (city) residents are heard by Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1 at 210 West Davis Street in the Lee G. Alworth Building in downtown Conroe — about a 25-minute drive east of the city of Montgomery. The court is one of only a handful of dedicated statutory probate courts outside Texas's largest urban counties, and uncontested administrations generally close in 4 to 6 months.

Whether you're administering a parent's estate, dealing with lakefront property, or putting an estate plan in place, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

localFaqs.question: I live in the city of Montgomery — do I file probate there or in Conroe?
localFaqs.answer: In Conroe. Despite the shared name, the city of Montgomery is not the county seat — Conroe is. The only probate court in Montgomery County is Probate Court No. 1, located at 210 West Davis Street in the Lee G. Alworth Building in Conroe, about 25 minutes east of the city of Montgomery. localFaqs.question: I retired to Lake Conroe from another state. Is my old will still valid in Texas?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes, if it was validly executed under your prior state's law. But Texas has features that an out-of-state will rarely takes advantage of — independent administration, community property treatment, the Transfer on Death Deed, and the Muniment of Title shortcut. We typically recommend a Texas-specific will or trust update once you've established residency.

localFaqs.question: We own a lakefront home on the west side of Lake Conroe. Anything different about that?
localFaqs.answer: Lakefront property in Montgomery County passes through probate the same way any other home does — but it's worth confirming any associated boat slip or shared waterway interest is properly documented in the deed. If the home is held jointly with right of survivorship, or covered by a Texas Transfer on Death Deed, it can pass outside of probate entirely. We can review the deed and tell you which path applies.

localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for Waller County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Waller County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested probate in Waller County typically closes in 3 to 5 months. Smaller rural counties often move faster than metropolitan Harris because the docket carries less volume.

localFaqs.question: We own a working farm. Should we put it in a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes — particularly when more than one heir will inherit, or when the operation needs to continue uninterrupted after a death. A revocable living trust holding the land, equipment, and livestock can avoid probate entirely and keep the operation running through the transition. We can sit down with you and walk through whether a trust makes sense for your specific land and family circumstances.

probate-attorney-prairie-view-tx.heroIntro: Prairie View is home to Prairie View A&M University — an HBCU with deep roots, a substantial faculty and educator population, and a community where retirement accounts and university benefits are often central to estate planning. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles Waller County probate for Prairie View families with over 25 years of Texas experience.
probate-attorney-prairie-view-tx.localContent:
Prairie View is shaped, more than almost any small Texas city, by its university. Prairie View A&M University was founded in 1876, is one of the nation's oldest historically Black universities, and remains the economic and cultural anchor of the community. The city's population is a mix of university faculty and staff, students living off-campus, longtime Waller County families, and retirees — many of whom spent careers in education, public service, or the petrochemical industry along the Gulf Coast. That gives our estate planning conversations in Prairie View a distinctive character. We frequently work with educators and university retirees who have substantial TRS (Teacher Retirement System) benefits, ORP (Optional Retirement Program) accounts, 403(b) plans, and life insurance through the university — all of which pass by beneficiary designation and require careful attention. We also see longtime landowning families with multi-generational property interests in the surrounding agricultural Waller County area.

Prairie View is in Waller County, so probate matters are heard by the Waller County Court — the constitutional county court — at the courthouse, 836 Austin Street in Hempstead, about a 15-minute drive west of Prairie View. Filings are made through the Waller County Clerk. Waller's docket is smaller than the metropolitan counties, and uncontested matters typically close in 3 to 5 months.

Whether you're administering a parent's estate, navigating university retirement benefits, or putting a plan in place, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Prairie View Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Waller County Court:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Waller County
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Prairie View homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — used for Waller County family land where probate was never opened
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Prairie View Faculty, Staff, and Retirees

For university employees and retirees, beneficiary designations are doing a lot of the work. We help Prairie View residents with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families, multi-state property, and structuring intergenerational wealth transfer
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps a Prairie View home or family land out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for TRS, ORP, 403(b), and university life insurance — areas where outdated designations naming an ex-spouse or deceased relative are common and easily fixed

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Waller County

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in Waller County. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Prairie View families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Prairie View and Waller County

Our office serves clients throughout Prairie View, Hempstead, Waller, Brookshire, Pattison, and the surrounding Waller and northwest Harris County communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Prairie View residents?
localFaqs.answer: Prairie View is in Waller County, so probate matters are heard by the constitutional county court at the Waller County Courthouse, 836 Austin Street, Hempstead, TX 77445 — about 15 minutes west of Prairie View. Filings are made through the Waller County Clerk; the clerk's office phone is (979) 826-7711.

localFaqs.question: My spouse worked at Prairie View A&M for 30 years and had TRS benefits and a 403(b). Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Generally no. TRS retirement benefits, ORP accounts, 403(b) plans, and university life insurance all pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning they bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. The most common problem we see is outdated designations: a beneficiary named decades ago who's an ex-spouse, has predeceased, or wasn't updated after a remarriage. Reviewing those designations is one of the simplest and highest-value pieces of planning we do for Prairie View educators.

localFaqs.question: Our family has owned land outside of Prairie View for several generations. Can we put it in a trust to pass to our kids?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, and for many families with multi-generational land that's the right move. A revocable living trust holding the acreage avoids probate entirely at death, lets you specify how the land should be divided or kept whole among heirs, and can include conditions for ongoing use. We can sit down with you and walk through whether a trust makes sense for your specific land and family circumstances.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Waller County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested probate in Waller County typically closes in 3 to 5 months. Smaller rural counties often move faster than metropolitan Harris because the docket carries less volume.
